1:
On Matthew 13:1 — “the same day went Jesus out of the house, and sat by the sea side” — the witnesses read the setting of the seven parables.

The Latin and eastern fathers find “heavenly sacraments” even in the place. Chrysostom notes Christ “departed out of the house” after correcting his brethren’s “desire of vainglory,” and sat so “that He should have none behind Him, but all… before His face.” Rabanus reads the move as a figure — “having left Judaea because of their… unbelief, He would pass to the salvation of the Gentiles,” the ship a type of the Church gathered “in the midst of the nations.” Hilary agrees: “the ship offers a type of the Church, within which the word of life is placed,” preached to those “without” who, “as being barren sand, cannot understand it.” Jerome pictures Jesus “in the midst of the waves… secure in His majesty,” teaching the people on the shore “what they were not worthy to hear within.”

The Puritan and grammatical-historical commentators mark his unwearied zeal and humble circumstance. Henry notes it was “the same day” as the foregoing sermon — Christ “for preaching both ends of the day” (Ecclesiastes 11:6) — and that in his “state of humiliation” he “denied himself the honours due to him,” having “not a chapel of his own to preach in,” his pulpit “a ship… converted to this use for want of a better.” Robertson calls it “the Busy Day,” the crowds “stretched up and down” the shore — “a picturesque scene.” Where Christ is, Henry adds, “there is the church, though it be by the sea-side.”

The witnesses make the day’s shift to the shore a lesson in following the word. Henry notes that “those who would get good by the word must be willing to follow it in all its removes; when the ark shifts, shift after it” — the Pharisees had laboured “to drive the people off from following Christ, but they still flocked after him.” Chrysostom adds that the very phrase “the sower went out” speaks of the Incarnation: “whence then went out He who is everywhere present…? not in place, but by His incarnation being brought nearer to us by the garb of the flesh” — “forasmuch as we because of our sins could not enter in unto Him, He therefore came forth to us.” He begins with the sower, Chrysostom observes, “to make His hearers more attentive,” since he “was about to speak enigmatically”; for even when the most part of the seed perishes, that came “not of him that sowed, but of the soil that received it, that is the mind.”
2:
On Matthew 13:2 — “and great multitudes were gathered together unto him, so that he went into a ship… and the whole multitude stood on the shore” — the witnesses note the crowd, the boat, and the posture.

The grammatical-historical commentators picture the scene. Gill observes the multitudes came “on divers accounts” — some to see his person, some his miracles, some for the loaves, some to hear — “of these there were several sorts, as the… parable shows.” Lightfoot notes the Jewish custom that “the masters when they read their lectures sat, and the scholars stood,” an “honorary custom” reversed only after Gamaliel’s death. Robertson and Clarke explain the boat — “the vessel” that seems to have been kept “for the use of Christ and his apostles” (compare Luke 5:3) — Jesus taking his seat in it “because of the crush of the crowd” on the beach where “the waves break one after the other.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out the design of the assembly. The Evangelists begin by telling that “a vast multitude had assembled,” all “eager to hear, but not equally desirous to receive instruction”; the parable was “to inform them, that the seed of doctrine… is not everywhere productive, because it does not always find a fertile… soil.” Christ “was there in the capacity of a husbandman” going out to sow. So, Calvin adds, “we need not wonder if, in our own day, the Gospel does not yield fruit in many,” of whom “some are lazy… others hear with indifference… and others are scarcely drawn even to hear.” Quesnel’s figure, noted by Clarke, sees in the ship and shore “a representation of the Church,” her pastors “more exposed to… storms.”

The witnesses draw out the humility and the design of the scene. Henry notes the people “stood upon dry ground and firm ground, while the Preacher was upon the water in more hazard” — “ministers are most exposed to trouble.” Quesnel’s figure, which Clarke records, sees in it “a representation of the Church, which consists of the people united to their pastors”; the pastors, “more exposed to violent tossings and storms,” are “in a ship, while those continue at ease on the shore.” Calvin presses the parable’s aim from the very crowd: that “the seed of doctrine… is not everywhere productive, because it does not always find a fertile… soil” — many ran to Christ “like hungry persons,” yet are “compared to an unproductive and barren soil.” The picture, all agree, is set down to prepare the hearer to ask in which soil his own heart belongs.
3:
On Matthew 13:3 — “and he spake many things unto them in parables, saying, Behold, a sower went forth to sow” — the witnesses explain the method of parables and open the first of them.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the form. A parable, Barnes notes, is “a similitude taken from a natural object to illustrate a spiritual… subject” — not necessarily a true narrative, “the main thing… gained equally” either way — designed to “convey truth in a more interesting manner,” to “arrest the attention of ignorant people,” to drive home “some offensive truth” (as Nathan’s, 2 Samuel 12:7), and to “conceal from one part of his audience truths… others should understand.” Lightfoot and Clarke note how familiar the form was among the Jews — “their very religion might be called parabolical” — yet “not one of” Christ’s “comes through his hands without being astonishingly improved.” Robertson distinguishes parable, fable, and allegory, warning that “much heresy has come from fantastic interpretations.” Ellicott pictures the Eastern field — “the road passing across… the rock… under an inch or two of soil… the patch of good ground.”

The witnesses note the fitness of the figure. Henry observes Christ’s parables are “borrowed from common, ordinary things… within the reach of the meanest capacity,” that “spiritual things… might… slide into our understandings,” and that “common actions might… be spiritualized.” Wesley marks the sower as “exquisitely proper… an introduction to all the rest,” answering “why” the same seed “has not always the same effect.” The mustard-seed material attached here by the Catena — the kingdom “the least of all seeds” grown to a tree — belongs to a later parable (Matthew 13:31) and stands as a stray.

The witnesses dwell on the wisdom and the warning of teaching by parables. Wesley notes the method “drew and fixed the attention of many… by an awful mixture of justice and mercy, it hid them from the proud and careless.” Robertson warns that “much heresy has come from fantastic interpretations”: “as a rule the parables of Jesus illustrate one main point, and the details are more or less incidental” — so “we should be slow to interpret the minor details” where Christ does not. Jerome marks that “this is the first parable that has been given with its interpretation,” and bids us “beware where the Lord expounds His own teaching, that we do not presume to understand any thing… otherwise than as so expounded by Him.” Henry adds that Christ “condescended to the capacities of people, and lisped to them in their own language” — clothing heavenly things “with expressions borrowed from things earthly” (John 3:12).
4:
On Matthew 13:4 — “and when he sowed, some seeds fell by the way side, and the fowls came and devoured them up” — the witnesses describe the first and least fruitful soil.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground the image in Eastern husbandry. Lightfoot notes the “ways and paths… along the sown fields” (compare Matthew 12:1); Barnes, that the wayside was “the hard path… which the plough had not touched,” where the seed “never entered.” Ellicott pictures the surface “hard and smooth, the grain lay on the surface, the pigeons and other birds that followed the sower reaped an immediate harvest.” Robertson notes people “will make paths along the edge of a ploughed field or even across it where the seed lies upon the beaten track,” the birds “ate down” the seed.

The witnesses draw the lesson of the careless hearer. Henry (whose exposition runs through the parable) marks the wayside as those who “hear the word and understand it not… it comes in at one ear and goes out at the other.” “The wicked one… the devil” then “catcheth away that which was sown,” for such “mindless… hearers are an easy prey to Satan,” who is “the great thief of sermons.” Wesley notes the order of the hindrances — the birds first, then the scorching, then the thorns — and that “a vast majority of those who hear the word of God receive the seed as by the highway side.” If we “break not up the fallow ground” by preparing the heart, and “cover not the seed… by meditation and prayer,” we are as the trodden path.

The witnesses press the remedy against the wayside heart. The seed perishes there, Henry notes, because the ground was “neither ploughed before nor harrowed after”: if we “break not up the fallow ground, by preparing our hearts… and humbling them to it,” and “cover not the seed afterwards, by meditation and prayer,” we are as the trodden path. The careless hearer “is thinking of something else, when he should be thinking of the things that belong to his peace,” and so is “an easy prey to Satan… the great thief of sermons.” Wesley marks the order of the dangers Christ names — the birds first, then the scorching, then the thorns — “our Lord points out the grand hindrances of our bearing fruit in the same order as they occur,” and notes that in none of the cases “is it the will of God that hinders, but their own voluntary perverseness.”
5:
On Matthew 13:5 — “some fell upon stony places, where they had not much earth: and forthwith they sprung up, because they had no deepness of earth” — the witnesses describe the second soil.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the ground. Barnes and Ellicott take it as “a thin stratum of earth” over “solid rock”; Robertson notes that “in that limestone country ledges of rock often jut out with thin layers of soil.” The seed “sprang up” the sooner — “not that they sprouted… faster, but… not so long in reaching the surface” (Barnes), “shot up at once” (Robertson) — the very rapidity that “was afterwards fatal” (Ellicott), the rock making the heat more intense and giving no room for root. Gill reads the stony ground of those in whom “the natural hardness of their hearts continues… without any true sense of sin.”

The witnesses dwell on the deceptive quickness of such hearers. Henry notes “hypocrites often get the start of true Christians in the shows of profession, and are often too hot to hold”: they receive the word “straightway, without trying it… swallow it without chewing,” and “receive it with joy” — for “many are very glad to hear a good sermon, that yet do not profit by it.” The heart “may melt under the word, and yet not be melted down by the word.” Such “endure for awhile, like a violent motion, which continues as long as the impression… remains,” but “do not endure to the end” (Matthew 10:22).

The witnesses warn against a profession that outruns its root. Henry notes such hearers “are quick in hearing, swift to hear”: the seed “sooner appeared above ground than that which was sown in the good soil,” for “hypocrites often get the start of true Christians in the shows of profession, and are often too hot to hold.” He receives the word “straightway, without trying it; swallows it without chewing, and then there can never be a good digestion” — whereas those “most likely to hold fast” are they that “prove all things” (1 Thessalonians 5:21). The joy itself is no proof: “many taste the good word of God” (Hebrews 6:5) and find sweetness in it, “but some beloved lust… is rolled under the tongue… and so they spit it out again.” The lesson, all agree, is that emotion without depth of earth cannot last.
6:
On Matthew 13:6 — “and when the sun was up, they were scorched; and because they had no root, they withered away” — the witnesses read the failure of the rootless profession.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the wilting. Ellicott notes Luke’s phrase, “because they lacked moisture” (Luke 8:6): “the growth had been over-rapid,” and the underlying rock “at once made the heat more intense, and deprived the plant of the conditions of resistance.” Gill takes the scorching sun to denote “some sore temptation, or severe affliction, or fiery trial of persecution” — not the Gospel’s comfortable light — for “the word by these hearers is first received with joy.”

The Puritan Henry draws out the want of root. Such hearers “have no root in themselves, no settled, fixed principles… nothing firm that will be either the sap or the strength of their profession”: “it is possible there may be the green blade of a profession, where yet there is not the root of grace.” When “tribulation and persecution arise because of the word, he is offended” (Galatians 5:11). The same sun that “warms and cherishes that which was well rooted, withers… that which wanted root”; the cross of Christ, “to some a savour of life unto life, to others a savour of death.” A profession “taken up without consideration is commonly let fall without it: lightly come, lightly go” — a ship “without ballast” that fails “in stress of weather.”

The witnesses make the scorching sun the trial that reveals the rootless. The same sun, Henry observes, “which warms and cherishes that which was well rooted, withers and burns up that which wanted root” — as “the cross of Christ… is to some a savour of life unto life, to others a savour of death.” “After a fair gale of opportunity usually follows a storm of persecution, to try who have received the word in sincerity.” The want of root is the want of “settled, fixed principles”: a profession “taken up without consideration is commonly let fall without it — lightly come, lightly go.” Such a soul is “a ship without ballast,” which, “though she may at first out-sail the laden vessel, yet will… fail in stress of weather, and never make her port.” Gill takes the sun for “some sore temptation… or fiery trial of persecution,” not the comfortable light of the Gospel, since the word was first “received with joy.”
7:
On Matthew 13:7 — “and some fell among thorns; and the thorns sprung up, and choked them” — the witnesses describe the third soil, the most insidious.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the hidden roots. Ellicott: the thorns “were not visible at the time of sowing” — the ground “cleared, but the roots… left below the surface” — so that their growth and the grain’s “went on simultaneously, and ended in the survival… of the strongest,” the ears “slowly strangled.” Robertson notes the thorns “came up out of the ground, the seeds… already in the soil,” and “choked them off.” Gill and Clarke explain the field was “not cleared” of the briars within it.

The Puritan Henry expounds the two thorns Christ names. First, “the cares of this world”: “care for another world would quicken the springing of this seed, but care for this world chokes it” — worldly cares “entangling, vexing, scratching, and their end is to be burned” (Hebrews 6:8) — so that “those who are careful and cumbered about many things commonly neglect the one thing needful.” Second, “the deceitfulness of riches”: it is “not so much riches, as the deceitfulness of riches,” that does the mischief, when men “rely upon them, and… take an inordinate complacency in them.” Prosperity, Henry warns, “destroys the word in the heart… more dangerously” than persecution, “because more silently”: “the stones spoiled the root, the thorns spoil the fruit.”

The witnesses warn that prosperity chokes more silently than persecution. The thorns, Henry notes, “are a good guard to the corn when they are in the hedge, but a bad inmate when they are in the field”; “prosperity destroys the word in the heart, as much as persecution does; and more dangerously, because more silently — the stones spoiled the root, the thorns spoil the fruit.” Worldly cares “eat up that vigour of soul which should be spent in divine things; divert us from duty, distract us in duty.” And riches deceive only when trusted: “they cannot be said to be deceitful to us unless we put our confidence in them.” Wesley heaps up the charge — riches “put out the eyes, harden the heart, steal away all the life of God… and all the while are eagerly desired… even by those who believe there is a God.” The thorns’ hidden roots, Ellicott notes, ensure “the survival, not of the fittest, but of the strongest.”
8:
On Matthew 13:8 — “but other fell into good ground, and brought forth fruit, some an hundredfold, some sixtyfold, some thirtyfold” — the witnesses read the one fruitful soil.

The grammatical-historical commentators note both the fruit and its degrees. Gill takes the good ground for “good, honest-hearted hearers” made so “by the Spirit and grace of God.” The hundredfold, Ellicott and Robertson agree, was “not an exaggeration” — the standard of “a more than usually prosperous harvest,” as in Isaac’s tillage (Genesis 26:12) — though the different returns “imply that even here there were different degrees of fertility.” Clarke marvels, from his own agricultural experiments, at “the power of grain to multiply itself,” on which “God… has… impressed… an idea of his own infinity.”

The Puritan Henry makes fruitfulness the mark that “distinguished this good ground from the rest.” The good hearers are “intelligent” — they “understand… their own concern” in the word — and “fruitful,” for “we then bear fruit, when we practise according to the word.” Christ “does not say that this good ground has no stones… or no thorns,” but “none that prevailed to hinder its fruitfulness”; for “saints, in this world, are not perfectly free from the remains of sin, but happily freed from the reign of it.” Among the fruitful “some are more fruitful than others”; yet those who bring forth “but thirty-fold shall be graciously accepted of God,” their fruit “abounding to their account.”

The witnesses make fruitfulness the one mark that proves the soil. “Though there are many that receive the grace of God… in vain,” Henry notes, “yet God has a remnant by whom it is received to good purpose; for God’s word shall not return empty” (Isaiah 55:11). The good ground “has stones in it… and thorns,” but “none that prevailed to hinder its fruitfulness” — for “saints, in this world, are not perfectly free from the remains of sin, but happily freed from the reign of it.” The degrees — hundred, sixty, thirty — teach that “among fruitful Christians, some are more fruitful than others”; “we should aim at the highest degree” (John 15:8), yet those who bring forth “but thirty-fold shall be graciously accepted of God.” Clarke’s wonder at grain’s power to multiply turns to worship: God “has, in some sort, impressed on it an idea of his own infinity.”
9:
On Matthew 13:9 — “who hath ears to hear, let him hear” — the witnesses take the formula as a summons to attention and self-examination.

The grammatical-historical commentators note its force. Gill observes Christ used it “when anything of moment was delivered, and not so easy to be understood,” to “quicken the attention” and stir “a desire… to understand” — meaning ears given “to hear, so as to understand.” Clarke applies it: “let every person who feels the necessity of being instructed… pay attention… and he shall become wise unto salvation.” Robertson finds it “comforting to teachers… that even Jesus had to exhort people to listen and to understand his sayings,” which “will bear the closest thought and are often enigmatical.” Ellicott notes the formula was “probably familiar in the schools of the Rabbis.”

The Reformed Calvin reads in the words a distinction among hearers. They were intended “partly to show that all were not endued with true understanding… and partly to arouse his disciples to consider attentively” a doctrine “not readily… understood by all.” Christ “makes a distinction among the hearers, by pronouncing some to have ears, and others to be deaf”; and if it be asked how the former come to have ears, “Scripture testifies… that it is the Lord who” gives them, “and that no man obtains… this by his own industry” (Psalm 40:6). The misattached Cyril fragment, on the baptismal anointing of the ears, sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses press the formula as a charge to weigh and apply what is heard. Gill notes Christ used it for things “of great importance… not so easy to be understood,” that the hearers might “seriously consider… and diligently attend” — an effect it had on the disciples, who sought the interpretation. Clarke draws the promise: “let every person who feels the necessity of being instructed… pay attention… and he shall become wise unto salvation.” The word, all agree, demands not the bodily ear only but “ears of the mind, to understand… and do those things which are commanded” (Remigius). Calvin grounds the distinction in grace: if any have ears to hear, “it is the Lord who” bores them (Psalm 40:6), “and no man obtains… this by his own industry” — so the summons is also a call to seek the hearing heart from God, who alone gives it.
10:
On Matthew 13:10 — “and the disciples came, and said unto him, Why speakest thou unto them in parables?” — the witnesses note the disciples’ kindly question and the reason Christ gives.

The fathers admire the manner and matter of the question. Chrysostom notes they asked “not before the multitude” but “privately” (Mark 4:10), and with “goodness” — “they say not, Why speakest thou to us… but to them,” seeking others’ profit first. Christ’s answer, “to you it is given to know the mysteries,” implies “no necessity or fate,” but shows “the knowledge of the Divine mysteries is the gift of God” while “they, to whom it is not given, are the cause of all their own miseries.” The blindness, Chrysostom insists, “is voluntary”: he says not “they see not,” but “seeing they see not” (Isaiah 6:9), for “they spake the very contrary to what they saw and heard.”

The Latin and Puritan commentators wrestle with the deeper mystery. Augustine, at length, holds that the Jews “by some… sins had… deserved to be blinded,” yet that the parables’ obscurity was meant “in mercy” — that, humbled at last “by the guilt of His death,” they “might turn… with a more healthy penitence” (reconciling John 12:39). Henry sums the double effect: “a parable, like the pillar of cloud and fire, turns a dark side towards Egyptians… but a light side towards Israelites” — “a shell that keeps good fruit for the diligent, but keeps it from the slothful.” To the disciples “it is given… to know,” for “the nearer we draw to Christ… the better acquainted we shall be with gospel mysteries”; from the willingly ignorant, in righteous judgment, “the light” is taken away.

The witnesses commend the disciples’ question and unfold the great answer. Henry notes they asked “not, Why speakest thou to us? (they knew how to get the parables explained) but to them” — teaching us to be “concerned for the edification of others.” The reason Christ gives is twofold: the parable “turns a dark side towards Egyptians… but a light side towards Israelites,” serving “a double intention.” To the willingly ignorant, “it is just with God to take away the light from those who shut their eyes against it”; to the disciples, “the nearer we draw to Christ… the better acquainted we shall be with gospel mysteries.” On the deepest point the witnesses divide — Chrysostom holding the blindness wholly “voluntary” (“seeing they see not”), Augustine wrestling that it was judicial for prior sins yet meant “in mercy” that they might at last “turn… with a more healthy penitence” — both agreeing that “God is without fault” and the guilt the people’s own.
11:
On Matthew 13:11 — “because it is given unto you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given” — the witnesses define “mystery” and trace the distinction to God’s grace.

The grammatical-historical commentators correct a common misreading. A “mystery,” Barnes notes, is “a thing that is concealed… not… incomprehensible” — here “doctrines about the preaching of the gospel… which had not been understood” (compare Romans 16:25; Ephesians 3:9), such as the call of the Gentiles and the death of the Messiah. Ellicott adds it means not “something awfully obscure” but a truth “kept a secret from others” yet “revealed to the initiated”; Robertson traces the word to mystēs, “one initiated” — the disciples “initiated into the secrets of the kingdom,” the parables “open to the disciples, but shut to the Pharisees with their hostile minds.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out the double design of preaching. Christ “intentionally spoke obscurely, in order that his discourse might be a riddle to many”; yet “the word of God, in its own nature, is always bright… its light is choked by the darkness of men.” God “conceals its mysteries, so that the perception of them may not reach the reprobate” (2 Corinthians 2:16); and the comparison is meant “to exhibit the magnitude of the grace bestowed on his disciples,” whose privilege has no cause “in themselves” — Christ “excludes all merit,” for “God calls to himself those whom he has gratuitously elected.” Wesley keeps it plain: “to them who have not, it is not given; therefore speak I in parables.”

The witnesses guard the doctrine of the “mysteries” on both sides. They are not, Barnes insists, “doctrines incomprehensible in themselves” but truths “not… understood” till revealed — the call of the Gentiles, the death of the Messiah — given to the disciples because “they were to preach it” and to “carry the gospel around the globe.” Yet the gift is sovereign: Calvin finds “no reason… in themselves,” for Christ, by saying “it is given,” “excludes all merit,” God calling “those whom he has gratuitously elected.” Clarke adds that the knowledge “in the first instance can be given only to a few,” but when these “faithfully write and publish what they have heard… the science of salvation is revealed… to all” — the choice of the persons and the time of revelation resting always “with the all-wise God.”
12:
On Matthew 13:12 — “for whosoever hath, to him shall be given… but whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken away even that he hath” — the witnesses read the proverbial rule of God’s dispensing.

The grammatical-historical commentators take it as a maxim of spiritual growth. “A man who improves what light, grace, and opportunities he has, shall have them increased” (Barnes); Ellicott notes it teaches the same lesson as “the parables of the Talents and the Pounds” (Matthew 25:29), the faithless Jew “sinking down to the level of a superstitious heathen.” Clarke illustrates from Juvenal’s poor Codrus, who “all that Nothing lost,” and warns that “he who does not improve the first operations of grace… is in danger of losing… even the principal.” Wesley calls it “the grand rule of God’s dealing… fixed as the pillars of heaven.”

The Reformed Calvin grounds the promise in God’s unwearied bounty. The favours God bestows spring from “his contemplation of his own liberality”: “those whom he has once begun to form are continually polished more and more, till… brought to the highest perfection” (Psalm 138:8), “for it is his purpose to complete the work of our salvation already commenced.” The reprobate, by contrast, “are continually proceeding from bad to worse, till… they waste away in their own poverty”: their gifts “shine only in the eyes of men,” having “nothing of real value.” Hence, Calvin urges, “let us learn to aim at progress throughout our whole life,” God granting the taste of his doctrine “on the express condition” that we “feed on it abundantly from day to day.”

The witnesses press the proverb as both promise and warning, urging diligence. Clarke draws the searching point from Juvenal’s Codrus, who lost his very “Nothing”: “those who devote not the light and power which God has given them… from them shall be taken away these unemployed or prostituted blessings.” Calvin makes the increase a comfort: “whenever he advances us to a higher degree, let us remember that every increase… flows from this source, that it is his purpose to complete the work of our salvation already commenced.” The threat falls on the unprofitable: “God will call in his talents out of their hands that are likely to become bankrupts quickly” (Henry). The gifts of the reprobate, Calvin adds, “shine only in the eyes of men,” having “nothing of real value” within — so the rule bids each “aim at progress throughout… life.”
13:
On Matthew 13:13 — “therefore speak I to them in parables: because they seeing see not, and hearing they hear not” — the witnesses weigh whether the parable was a mercy or a judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it chiefly as penal, yet not unkind. Barnes notes Mark and Luke have “that seeing they may not see” — stating the intention — while Matthew “simply states the fact”; there was “no dishonesty,” for Christ stated his doctrines “in the best possible way,” and “it was not his fault if they did not understand.” Clarke presses that he spoke in parables “to lead them into a proper knowledge of God… that they may be allured to inquire” — for “who can suppose that he would… speak… on purpose that they might not understand?” Ellicott holds both: as the close of the proverb (v.12) shows, it was “the working of the divine law of retribution,” yet “beneath the penalty an element of mercy,” for the parable “could do men no harm” and “might set men thinking.”

The Reformed Calvin refers it to “the secret purpose of God.” Christ speaks obscurely to the multitude “because they are not partakers of the true light”; yet, declaring “that a veil is spread over the blind,” he “does not ascribe the blame… to themselves” here, but “takes occasion to commend more highly the grace bestowed on the Apostles,” assigning no cause “except the secret purpose of God.” Wesley sums the practical sense: “I do not give more knowledge to this people, because they use not that which they have already.”

The witnesses hold together the justice and the mercy of the parabolic veil. Clarke recoils from the harsher reading: “who can suppose that he would employ his time in speaking enigmatically… on purpose that they might not understand?” — rather Christ spoke so “that they may be allured to inquire, and to find out the spirit… hidden under the letter.” Barnes balances it: had Christ “clearly… stated” his truths, “they would have taken his life long before they did”; by parables he “prepared many… to receive the truth… by the only possible way of ever gaining access to their minds.” Ellicott finds even in the penalty “an element of mercy,” the parable being unable to “rouse the fierce enmity” that plain truth had kindled, and able to “set men thinking… one step towards the having.” Wesley keeps the moral root: “I do not give more knowledge… because they use not that which they have.”
14:
On Matthew 13:14 — “and in them is fulfilled the prophecy of Esaias… By hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand” — the witnesses note the standing of this much-quoted prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the manner of fulfilment. The quotation is from Isaiah 6:9–10, “literally fulfilled in the time of Isaiah” and again in Christ’s, the people having “the same character” (Barnes). Clarke renders the verb “is again fulfilled”: as the words “were fulfilled in the Jews… so they are now again fulfilled in… their posterity.” Robertson and Ellicott note Matthew here points out the fulfilment directly, not with his usual formula, and that these words, quoted also by John (John 12:40) and Paul (Acts 28:26), “dwelt on the minds of the Master and His disciples,” preparing them for “the seeming fruitlessness” of their work.

The Reformed Calvin gathers the New Testament’s varied uses of the text. Paul in Acts charges the Jews with “obstinate malice” — the immediate cause in the men; but in Romans 11:7 he draws “from a deeper… source,” that the elect obtain mercy “and all others perish by a hidden, though just, judgment of God.” The blame, Calvin insists, “rests wholly on themselves, because this blindness is voluntary”; yet the ministers of the word may take consolation — if their labour does not always succeed, “what has befallen them was experienced by a Prophet… to whom they are not superior.” Wesley: “all possible means will be given you: yet they will profit you nothing; because your heart is… insensible.”

The witnesses note how this prophecy haunted the apostolic mind. Quoted by Christ, by John (John 12:40), and by Paul (Acts 28:26), it is, Ellicott says, “as though the words which sounded at the very opening of Isaiah’s prophecy as the knell of the nation’s life dwelt on the minds of the Master and His disciples.” Henry marks that it “is referred to no less than six times in the New Testament,” intimating “that in gospel times spiritual judgments would be most common, which make least noise, but are most dreadful.” The saddest condition “a man can be in on this side hell” is “to sit under the most lively ordinances with a dead, stupid, untouched heart.” Calvin draws the consolation for ministers: if their labour fail, “what has befallen them was experienced by a Prophet” — yet bids them be “extremely careful that the fruit of the Gospel be not lost through our negligence.”
15:
On Matthew 13:15 — “for this people’s heart is waxed gross… lest… they should… be converted, and I should heal them” — the witnesses describe the wilful blindness and its just penalty.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the figures. “Waxed gross” is literally “has become fat” (Barnes) — the heart “callous or dull, even fatty degeneration” (Robertson); the closed eyes were “smeared over” or sealed, “an oriental punishment” (Robertson; Isaiah 29:10). The fault, all agree, lay in the people: it points to “the obstinate, wilful ignorance which refuses to look on the truth, lest the look should lead to conviction” (Ellicott; John 3:19) — “the fault is here totally in the people, and not at all in… God” (Clarke).

The Reformed Calvin dwells on the “healing.” The word includes “deliverance from every evil,” the afflicted people compared to a sick man; and this healing “depends on the pardon of sins,” for “whence comes the mitigation of chastisements, but because God has been reconciled?” He guards the order carefully: “we must not… conclude that repentance is the cause of pardon, as if God received… men because they deserved it” — rather “repentance itself is a mark of God’s free favor” — the verse expressing only “that God does not forgive the sins of any but those who are dissatisfied with themselves.” The misattached Cyril fragment, on the freedom of the will and the closed eyes, sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses trace the chain from sin to judgment and back to mercy. The blindness is the people’s own: “they shut their ears… their eyes also they have closed… because they loved darkness rather than light” (Henry; John 3:19). The judicial penalty answers the sin: “it is just with God to give an understanding heart, and he often, in a way of righteous judgment, denies it to those to whom he has given the hearing ear… in vain.” Yet the verse ends in the open door of healing: “if they be converted, I shall heal them” — so that “if sinners perish, it is not to be imputed to God, but to themselves; they foolishly expected to be healed, without being converted.” Calvin guards the order: the healing “depends on the pardon of sins,” and repentance, far from earning it, is itself “a mark of God’s free favor” — God forgiving “none but those who are dissatisfied with themselves.”
16:
On Matthew 13:16 — “but blessed are your eyes, for they see: and your ears, for they hear” — the witnesses read the beatitude set against the blindness of the rest.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the happiness of the disciples. They were “permitted to see truth which” others “will not see” (Barnes), and “improve the light which God has given” (Clarke). Ellicott notes the words were spoken “to the small company… in the boat”: they might “see but dimly,” yet had “eyes that looked for light, and ears… open to the divine voice.” Gill dwells on their double blessing — to see Christ “in the flesh” and, more, to see “his personal and transcendent glories, as the Son of God,” where many saw him “with their bodily eyes” yet found “no beauty… in him.”

The Reformed Calvin guards the saying against misuse. Their blessedness is not “the mere sight of the flesh,” but that “they perceive in him a glory… worthy of the only-begotten Son of God,” fulfilling the promise that the truly taught of God “do not need to” be taught (Isaiah 54:13; Jeremiah 31:34). This is no contradiction of “blessed are they that have not seen, and yet have believed” (John 20:29) — that rebuked Thomas’s “gross apprehension” — for the sight Christ here commends “has been enjoyed by believers in every age”: “in the Gospel we behold him… face to face, so as to be transformed into his image” (2 Corinthians 3:18). The misattached JFB note on “all things delivered” sits here as a stray.

The witnesses make the beatitude the bright counterpart to the blindness. “It was to illustrate this blessedness,” Henry notes, “that Christ said so much of the misery of those who are left in ignorance.” The disciples’ eyes are blessed not for natural sight but because “the hearing ear and the seeing eye are God’s work in those who are sanctified… the work of his grace” (Proverbs 20:12); “the eyes of the meanest believer… are more blessed than those of the greatest… masters in… philosophy… that are strangers to God.” Calvin presses that this sight “has been enjoyed by believers in every age in common with the Apostles”: we “do not see Christ, and yet we see him… for in the Gospel we behold him… face to face, so as to be transformed into his image” (2 Corinthians 3:18). True blessedness, the witnesses conclude, “is entailed upon the right understanding… of the mysteries of the kingdom of God.”
17:
On Matthew 13:17 — “many prophets and righteous men have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them” — the witnesses read the disciples’ privilege above the saints of old.

The grammatical-historical commentators picture the longing of the prophets. They were “men of desires,” Ellicott notes, each standing “on a Pisgah height” looking on “a land which he was not to enter”; the words “have not seen them” are only verbally at odds with “Abraham rejoiced to see my day” (John 8:56), for there is “a joy in looking on the distant prospect” that “implies the desire to reach” it — the “searching diligently” of 1 Peter 1:10. Barnes: the prophets “looked to the coming of the Messiah as the consummation of their wishes… the end of the prophecies” (Hebrews 11:13).

The Puritan Henry calls it “a transcendent blessing, desired by, but not granted to, many… righteous men.” The Old-Testament saints “had the types, shadows, and prophecies… but longed to see the Substance”; their discoveries were made “according to the dispensation they are under,” for “God’s favours shall not anticipate his counsels.” The lesson, Henry adds, is for “the exciting of our thankfulness”: we should consider “what discoveries are made to us, now under the gospel, above what they… enjoyed” under the Old Testament (2 Corinthians 3:7), and see “that our improvements be proportionable to our advantages.” The misattached Cyril fragment, on the proofs of eternal life, sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses turn the disciples’ privilege into a call to thankfulness. The Old-Testament saints, Henry notes, “had some glimmerings of gospel light” and “coveted earnestly further discoveries”; they “desired to see the great Salvation… but did not see it, because the fulness of time was not yet come.” Two lessons follow: “those who know something of Christ cannot but covet to know more,” and the discoveries of grace are made to each “according to the dispensation they are under,” God’s favours not anticipating “his counsels.” And it is “for the exciting of our thankfulness” that we should weigh “what discoveries are made to us, now under the gospel, above what they… enjoyed” (Hebrews 11:40) — and see that “our improvements be proportionable to our advantages.” Ellicott’s image of the prophet on his Pisgah, looking on a land he could not enter, gathers the whole longing of the old economy.
18:
On Matthew 13:18 — “hear ye therefore the parable of the sower” — the witnesses note the privilege of the interpretation and the four kinds of soil.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators mark the gentle reproof. Mark adds, “Know ye not this parable? and how then will ye know all parables?” (Mark 4:13) — regarding it, Barnes notes, as “one of the simplest,” the key to interpreting the rest. The emphatic “ye” (Ellicott, Robertson) marks the interpretation “withheld from others” but given to the disciples. Calvin lists the “four kinds of hearers” — those who do not receive the seed, those in whom it takes no deep root, those in whom the corn is choked, and the fruitful — warning that Christ “makes no mention of despisers who openly reject the word,” but describes “those only in whom there is some appearance of docility”; “if the greater part of such men perish, what shall become of the rest of the world?”

The Latin fathers dwell on the threefold fruit and divide over its meaning. Jerome assigns “the hundred-fold fruit… to virgins, the sixty-fold to widows… the thirty-fold to chaste wedlock”; Augustine, with the martyrs at the hundredfold, reads the degrees as the contempt of death borne “by some with brave endurance, by others with content, and by others with gladness.” Remigius offers a different scheme — the thirty-fold of him who “teaches faith in the Holy Trinity,” the sixty-fold of good works, the hundred-fold of him who “promises eternal life” — or of good thoughts, speech, and works. The interpretation, all agree, is Christ’s own, and so to be received with reverence.

The witnesses note that to hear the interpretation is itself a grace. Mark’s reproof, “know ye not this parable?”, Calvin observes, blames the disciples “for being slow of apprehension, because those who were to be the teachers of all did not run before others.” Yet Christ kindly explains, “directing his discourse to the disciples, but in the hearing of the multitude” (Henry) — for “it is of good use… to hear over again what we have heard” (Philippians 3:1), since “then only we hear the word aright… when we understand what we hear.” The fathers’ varied schemes for the thirtyfold, sixtyfold, and hundredfold — whether of the married, widows, and virgins, or of degrees in the contempt of death, or of faith, works, and life — agree at least in this, that Christ owns degrees of fruit; and the interpretation, being his own, forbids us to “understand any thing… otherwise than as so expounded by Him.”
19:
On Matthew 13:19 — “when any one heareth the word of the kingdom, and understandeth it not, then cometh the wicked one, and catcheth away that which was sown in his heart” — the witnesses expound the first soil.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the seed and the enemy. The seed is “the word of the kingdom” (Matthew 4:23); “understandeth it not” means rather “regardeth it not, does not lay his heart to it” (Clarke). Clarke notes the three Evangelists each name the enemy differently — the “wicked one,” “Satan” the adversary, the “devil” who darts (compare Ephesians 6:16) — the careless heart being “the devil’s thoroughfare.” Ellicott observes that “everything which leads men to forget the truth is, in very deed, doing the work of the great enemy,” the birds in their multitude representing the Tempter’s “light and foolish thoughts.” Robertson: the devil “snatches… like a bandit… before it has time even to sprout.”

The Reformed Calvin reads the trodden soil. Such hearers “do not receive the seed within, because there is no preparation in their hearts” — “like what we find on a public road… trodden down, and… hard, like a pavement.” Yet their depravity does not make the word “to cease to have the character of seed”: “the Gospel is always a fruitful seed as to its power, but not as to its produce.” Luke’s addition — that “the wicked one” takes it away — shows that this “enemy of our salvation… watches and rushes forth to seize it, before it… springs up.” The Gloss notes that “that which is sown” is taken both of the seed and, in the man, of “the field sown by the seed of the Divine word.”

The witnesses make the wayside hearer a warning against inattention. The fault is moral, not intellectual: “understandeth it not” means “regardeth it not, does not lay his heart to it” (Clarke). The careless heart is “the devil’s thoroughfare” — “an open road, where evil affections… continually pass and repass, without either notice or restraint.” Robertson pictures the suddenness: “how quickly after the sermon the impression is gone,” the devil “busy with his job of snatching… the word of the kingdom before it has time even to sprout.” Calvin presses that the soil’s barrenness does not unmake the word: “the Gospel is always a fruitful seed as to its power, but not as to its produce” — “we must not suppose the favors of God to cease to be what they are, though the good effect of them does not reach us.” Ellicott reflects that it “reflects what had actually been our Lord’s experience”: every preacher since has known these same four issues of the seed.
20:
On Matthew 13:20 — “he that received the seed into stony places, the same is he that heareth the word, and anon with joy receiveth it” — the witnesses expound the second hearer.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the contrast with the first. Here is “rapid change, strong emotion, a quicker show of conversion” (Ellicott) — one “caught with” the gospel “as something new or pleasing… full of zeal” (Barnes), who “receiveth it with joy… perhaps with transport… struck with the beauty of truth” (Wesley). Gill notes such a one is “not an accidental hearer… but a settled, constant” one, with “some understanding of what he hears.” Ellicott observes that “in proportion to the tendency of any system… to cause excitement, are those results likely to be frequent.”

The Reformed Calvin defines the “temporary faith” here. It is “a sort of vegetation of the seed” that “promises at first some fruit”; but the hearts “are not so thoroughly subdued, as to have the softness necessary for their continued nourishment.” He warns from common experience of those who “eagerly embrace the Gospel, and shortly afterwards fall off”: “great commendation is due… to that promptitude, which receives the word… with joy… but let us learn, that nothing has been done, till faith acquires true firmness.” Lest the “alacrity” go out “like a fire of tow,” the word must “fully penetrate the whole heart, and strike its roots deep.”

The witnesses make the stony-ground hearer a caution against mistaking emotion for grace. Such men “are very glad to hear a good sermon” (Henry), “struck with the beauty of truth” (Wesley), yet “the heart may melt under the word, and yet not be melted down by the word.” Calvin bids each examine himself, “that the alacrity which gives out a bright flame may not quickly go out… like a fire of tow.” The remedy is depth: “if the word does not fully penetrate the whole heart, and strike its roots deep, faith will want the supply of moisture… for perseverance.” Ellicott’s note on the “revivalist meetings” that multiply such results is sobering — the more a method “causes excitement,” the more frequent the rootless joy. The lesson, all agree, is that genuine faith is known not by the speed of its springing but by its endurance.
21:
On Matthew 13:21 — “yet hath he not root in himself, but dureth for a while: for when tribulation or persecution ariseth… by and by he is offended” — the witnesses read the failure of the rootless.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the want of root. The “root,” Ellicott notes, is “the conviction which ripens into a purpose and strikes its fibres deep down into reason, conscience, and will”; Robertson likens the man to “a mushroom growth… quick to sprout, quick to stumble” — “a picture of some converts in our modern revivals… they did not have the root of the matter.” Clarke explains that his soul “is not deeply convinced of its guilt… the fallow ground is not properly ploughed up”; having “not felt his own sore,” he has “no motive in his heart strong enough to counteract the outward scandal of the cross.” Ellicott distinguishes “persecution” (“a more organised attack”) from “tribulation” (“the thousand petty annoyances”).

The Reformed Calvin makes the cross the test. “As the heat of the sun discovers the barrenness of the soil, so… the cross lays open the vanity of those who are slightly influenced.” Such persons “imagine that they have true faith,” rendering the word “an honor… which resembles faith”; yet “they are not truly regenerated by the incorruptible seed” (1 Peter 1:23). Wesley sums it: “no deep work of grace… no change in the ground of his heart… he finds a thousand plausible pretences for leaving so narrow and rugged a way.”

The witnesses press the lesson that the cross sifts the soul. Trial, Ellicott notes, was no rare thing in the early Church: even now “most men and women who seek to be Christians in deed… are at some time… exposed” to it. Robertson’s warning is blunt: such converts “drop away overnight because they did not have the root of the matter in them.” Calvin frames the deeper distinction: those who fall away “imagine that they have true faith,” but “none are partakers of true faith, except those who are sealed with the Spirit of adoption… and as that Spirit is never extinguished, so it is impossible that the faith which he has once engraven… shall pass away.” The rootless endure “for a while,” but the offence of the cross — “a stumbling-block in his way which he cannot get over” (Henry) — lays bare a profession that had no principle beneath it.
22:
On Matthew 13:22 — “he also that received seed among the thorns… the care of this world, and the deceitfulness of riches, choke the word” — the witnesses read the third and most insidious hearer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the divided heart. This hearer, unlike the stony, “has the capacity for sustained effort,” but “there is not unity of spirit… the man is double-minded, and would fain serve two masters” (Ellicott). There may be “the blade and the ear of partial reformation… but no full corn in the ear” — he brings “no fruit to perfection” (Luke 8:14). Robertson: “lust for money and care go together and between them spoil many an earnest religious nature.” Clarke unfolds merimna as “anxious, carking cares” that “distract the mind,” and the deceitfulness of riches, “which promise peace… but can never give it.”

The Reformed Calvin presses the universal danger. The sinful affections of the flesh “prevail over the hearts of men, and overcome faith… before it has reached maturity”; at first their influence “is not perceived,” appearing only “when the corn has grown up.” Each must “endeavor to tear the thorns out of his heart,” for “there is not one of us whose heart is not filled… with a thick forest” of them; yet “scarcely one individual out of ten” labours even “to cut down the thorns.” Riches are called “deceitful” on purpose, that men “may be more desirous to guard against falling into their snares” (1 Timothy 6:9). Wesley: riches “smile, and betray; kiss, and smite into hell.”

The witnesses make the thorns the subtlest peril of all. Robertson notes “the thorns flourish and the character sickens and dies, choked to death for lack of spiritual food, air, sunshine.” The double-mindedness is the heart of it: the man “would fain serve two masters” (Ellicott), and the cares and riches, growing “simultaneously” with the word, prevail. Calvin presses the universal application — “there is not one of us whose heart is not filled with… a thick forest of thorns” — and the slothfulness that lets them grow: “scarcely one individual out of ten… labors… even to cut down the thorns.” Wesley distributes the thorns: “cares are thorns to the poor; wealth to the rich; the desire of other things to all.” The remedy is daily war upon them, lest the word, which had grown to the blade and the ear, fail at last to bring “fruit to perfection.”
23:
On Matthew 13:23 — “but he that received seed into the good ground is he that heareth the word, and understandeth it; which also beareth fruit” — the witnesses read the one fruitful hearer.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the marks of the good ground. He “heareth… and understandeth” — not a merely intellectual act but a taking-in, with “an honest and good heart” (Luke), the word “received” and “kept” (Gill). Clarke names “to hear, to understand, and to bring forth fruit” as “the three grand evidences of a genuine believer.” Robertson: “the fruit reveals the character of the tree”; the first three soils “have no fruit… unsaved souls.” Ellicott adds the parable’s open question — “what made the ground good” it does not answer — and the practical caution that “the soil may be improved”: it is “the work of every preacher… to prepare the soil as well as to sow the seed” (Jeremiah 4:3).

The Reformed Calvin and the others note the degrees. The good ground is not the perfect but those in whom the word “overcomes every obstacle”; though “there will always be some” thorns remaining, “every one who does not fall off… is reckoned a good soil.” The differing returns — hundred, sixty, thirty — show that “all sound believers are not equally fruitful” (Clarke), yet the master “bestows the general designation even on inferior soils.” Calvin rejects Jerome’s scheme of virgins, widows, and married as an “absurd” torturing of the text, the piety of the married often yielding “more abundantly.”

The witnesses gather the parable’s practical edge. The good ground is no chance soil but a heart prepared: “in a rich and mellow soil… with the heart spread open… the gospel takes deep root, and grows” (Barnes). Clarke draws the chain — “he who does not hear… cannot understand… and he who is not fruitful… cannot be Christ’s disciple” (John 15:8) — yet adds that the bad ground’s barrenness “was not owing to bad seed or an unskilful sower,” but to the hearers’ own carelessness, and that any may cry to God to make the ground good. Ellicott’s caution rounds out the lesson: the parable “represents the phenomena of the spiritual life only partially,” for “the soil may be improved” — the wayside, the rock, and the thorny “may become as the good ground.” The preacher’s task is “to prepare the soil as well as to sow the seed.”
24:
On Matthew 13:24 — “the kingdom of heaven is likened unto a man which sowed good seed in his field” — the witnesses open the parable of the tares and divide on its great practical question.

The eastern and Latin fathers read the tares as heresy and the corrupt within the visible Church. Chrysostom: this is “the contrivance of the Devil, ever to mix error with truth” — error coming “after the truth,” as “false prophets after the Prophets… Antichrist after Christ.” On the command “Nay,” he draws the famous inference: “we ought not to kill an heretic, seeing that so a never-ending war would be introduced” — yet this “does not forbid all restraint… only… that they should be put to death.” Augustine, at great length, takes the tares for heretics (since “the field is… the world”), and wrestles with toleration: at first he held “no man was to be driven by force,” but later was “overcome… by the examples” of those whom imperial laws had recovered — “why should not the Church constrain her lost sons to return?” Jerome counsels patience, “lest… we should hastily cut off a brother,” since “one… today corrupted… may grow wiser tomorrow.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators state the parable’s aim. Calvin holds its design is to console the godly, who “perceive a confused mixture of the good along with the bad”: “so long as the pilgrimage of the Church… continues, bad men and hypocrites will mingle… that the children of God may be armed with patience.” Yet he limits the toleration to morals, not doctrine, and (against the Manichees) denies that the wicked are “created by the devil” — they are “created by God… corrupted by the devil.” Henry sums the scope: “the present and future state of the… gospel church… the mixture… of good and bad in the other world.”

The witnesses gather the abiding lessons of the parable’s opening. Rabanus reads in it a whole rule of pastoral wisdom: “an enemy came… that watch should be kept”; the tares suffered to grow, “he recommends patience”; “lest haply in gathering… ye root out the wheat,” he “sets us an example of discretion”; “suffer both to grow,” “long-suffering”; and “bind them in bundles to burn,” at last, “justice.” The deep practical debate — how the Church should deal with the corrupt within her — runs from Chrysostom’s “we ought not to kill an heretic” to Augustine’s reluctant defence of constraint, and Calvin’s careful line between tolerating bad lives and never tolerating “the wicked errors which corrupt the purity of faith.” All agree the parable’s first comfort is to the godly, “that the children of God may be armed with patience” amid “a confused mixture of the good along with the bad” in the field of the world.
25:
On Matthew 13:25 — “but while men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went his way” — the witnesses read the devil’s stealthy work.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the “tares.” They are not vetches but “darnel” (zizania), Robertson and Clarke explain — “a bastard wheat” or “degenerate wheat” (the rabbinic zunin), “indistinguishable from the wheat stalks” until the grain shows black — aptly representing “hypocrites in the church,” impossible to separate till “the great Searcher of hearts” does it (Barnes). Lightfoot notes all was sown with wheat, “one or the other,” the bad being “degenerate” wheat — good and bad “Christians” distinguished “among themselves.”

The witnesses dwell on the “sleep” and the “went his way.” Barnes: “Satan thus sows false doctrine in darkness… in the very place where the truth is preached,” often “in a revival of religion” spreading “dangerous notions of piety,” persuading multitudes “that they are Christians, who are deceived.” Having sown, the enemy “went his way” — knowing “the soil”: “error, and sin, and hypocrisy, are the native products of the human heart; and, when left alone, start up with deadly luxuriancy.” The danger is laid, says Clarke, both on the enemy and on “indolent pastors… who permit the souls under their care to be corrupted” while they sleep.

The witnesses press both the peril of the sleeping watchman and the cunning of the foe. “It is no small danger,” Chrysostom warns, “which He hereby suspends over our rulers, to whom… is entrusted the keeping of the field” — and not the rulers only, “but the subjects too.” Clarke turns it on negligent pastors: “woe to the indolent pastors who permit the souls under their care to be corrupted by error and sin.” The enemy’s craft is to counterfeit, not to invent: he sows “not… another seed, but tares, which in appearance are somewhat like wheat” (Chrysostom), so that error keeps “in the shade” at first and pours forth “its venom” only when grown. Henry adds that the tares, once sown, “will spring up of themselves” — “such is the proneness of fallen man to sin” — whereas the good seed “must be tended, watered, and fenced, or it will come to nothing.”
26:
On Matthew 13:26 — “but when the blade was sprung up, and brought forth fruit, then appeared the tares also” — the witnesses note when the hidden evil shows itself.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the time of discovery. The tares “had grown with the wheat, but so much like it as not to be noticed, till the wheat began to ripen” (Barnes); “the darnel became plain… by harvest” (Robertson). So “true piety and false hopes are not known by professions… but by fruit.” Clarke draws the revival-parallel: “Satan has a shoot of iniquity for every shoot of grace; and when God revives his work, Satan revives his also.” Wesley: “it seldom appears as soon as the good seed is sown; all at first appears to be peace, and love, and joy.”

The Puritan Henry draws out the hidden ripening of wickedness. “There is a great deal of secret wickedness in the hearts of men, which is long hid under the cloak of a plausible profession, but breaks out at last.” As both wheat and tares “lie a great while under the clods, and at first… it is hard to distinguish them,” so “when a trying time comes, when fruit is to be brought forth… then you will… discern between the sincere and the hypocrite.” The fruit-bearing of the wheat is precisely what brings the tares to light — the testing season revealing what the season of ease concealed. Gill reads the springing blade as the “profession of religion… which, when right, springs up from… a work begun in the heart.”

The witnesses make the late appearing of the tares a lesson on testing. The likeness held so long, Henry notes, that “at first… it is hard to distinguish them; but when a trying time comes… then you may say, This is wheat, and that is tares.” Chrysostom marks the same of the heretic: “at first they keep themselves in the shade; but when they have had long license… then they pour forth their venom.” The good and the false grow in the same field, under “the same rain, and sunshine, and soil” (Henry, elsewhere), yet are known at last by their fruit. The verse thus warns against premature judgment in either direction — the hidden tare not yet manifest, the true wheat not yet ripened — and against presuming on a profession that the season of ease has never tried. Gill reads the springing blade as the right profession of faith, which “ought to be held fast unto the end without wavering.”
27:
On Matthew 13:27 — “so the servants of the householder came and said unto him, Sir, didst not thou sow good seed in thy field? from whence then hath it tares?” — the witnesses read the servants’ grief and right resort.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the servants and their question. Gill takes them for “the ministers of the Gospel… who, observing the tares, and fearing the danger the wheat was in… came to him, and spread the case before him in prayer.” The question itself owns a settled truth: “whatever is amiss in the church, we are sure it is not of Christ” (Henry) — “considering the seed which Christ sows, we may well ask, with wonder, Whence should these tares come?” Clarke: “a faithful… minister… fails not to discover the evil, to lament it, and to address himself to God by prayer.”

The Puritan Henry dwells on the servants’ sorrow. “The rise of errors, the breaking out of scandals, and the growth of profaneness, are matter of great grief to all the servants of Christ; especially to his faithful ministers.” It is “sad to see such tares… in the garden of the Lord… and such a reflection cast on the… honour of Christ, as if his field were no better than the field of the slothful.” Their first recourse, the witnesses note, is not to act but to enquire of the Master — coming “not with the body, but with the heart and desire of the soul” (Remigius) — the right pattern for those grieved by the mixture in the Church.

The witnesses make the servants’ resort a model for the grieved. Their question, Gill notes, was “spread… before him in prayer” — they came “not with the body, but with the heart and desire of the soul” (Remigius), gathering from the Master himself that “an enemy hath done this.” The grief is right and faithful: “the rise of errors, the breaking out of scandals… are matter of great grief to all the servants of Christ” (Henry). And the confession in their question — “didst not thou sow good seed?” — clears the Master of all blame: “whatever is amiss in the church, we are sure it is not of Christ.” The pattern for the troubled, all agree, is neither to despair at the mixture nor to act rashly against it, but first to carry the case to Christ, whose field it is, and who alone knows how it shall be set right.
28:
On Matthew 13:28 — “he said unto them, An enemy hath done this. The servants said… Wilt thou then that we go and gather them up?” — the witnesses read the Master’s diagnosis and the servants’ hasty zeal.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the doctrine of evil’s origin. “An enemy hath done this” is, Wesley says, “a plain answer to the great question concerning the origin of evil”: God made men “free either to choose good or evil… but he implanted no evil… An enemy (with man’s concurrence) hath done this.” Gill notes the Hebraism “an enemy man” signifies “a certain enemy… an implacable enemy to man.” The Master, Henry observes, “does not lay the blame upon the servants… the ministers of Christ… shall not be judged… for the mixtures of bad with good” if they have done their duty.

The witnesses caution against the servants’ rash forwardness. Their wish to root up the tares at once is “the over-hasty and inconsiderate zeal of Christ’s servants, before they have consulted with their Master” (Henry) — “Lord, wilt thou that we call for fire from heaven?” Clarke: “a zeal which is rash and precipitate is as much to be feared as the total lack of strict discipline.” Wesley distinguishes the darnel (“outside Christians… the form… without the power”) from open sinners (“thistles and brambles”) who “ought to be rooted up without delay”; the difficulty lies precisely in the tares that resemble wheat. The misattached JFB note on the sabbath sits here as a stray.

The witnesses dwell on the answer to the origin of evil and the rebuke of rash zeal. “God made men (as he did angels) intelligent creatures… free either to choose good or evil,” Wesley notes, “but he implanted no evil… An enemy (with man’s concurrence) hath done this.” The Master’s calm diagnosis clears his diligent servants of blame (Henry). But their forwardness to uproot at once is checked: “the over-hasty and inconsiderate zeal of Christ’s servants, before they have consulted with their Master, is sometimes ready… to root out all that they presume to be tares.” The danger, Clarke warns, is mistaken identity — lest “a zeal… not regulated by these words… care not if they root up the wheat, provided they can but gather up the tares.” True discernment, the witnesses agree, leaves the doubtful case to the Lord of the harvest.
29:
On Matthew 13:29 — “but he said, Nay; lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them” — the witnesses read the Master’s patient wisdom.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the danger of premature uprooting. The tares “so much resembled the true wheat, that even then it would be difficult to separate them”; gathering them “would tread down the wheat… and greatly injure the crop” (Barnes), their roots “intermingled” (Robertson). Ellicott reads the householder as “a pattern of patient wisdom”: he “can defeat the malice of his foe, but he will choose his own time,” when “the difference of aspect” at harvest makes separation easy. Henry: “it is not possible for any man infallibly to distinguish between tares and wheat… therefore… Christ… will rather permit the tares, than any way endanger the wheat.”

The witnesses balance this against the duty of discipline. “Scandalous offenders are to be censured,” Henry grants, and the openly wicked “not… admitted to special ordinances”; yet discipline may be “so over-nice… as may prove vexatious to many that are truly godly,” so “great caution and moderation must be used” (2 Timothy 2:25). Clarke turns it against persecution: “men often persecute a true Christian, while they intend only to prosecute an impious person,” and “the zeal which leads persons to persecute… for religious opinions is… a seed of the devil.” The tares, all add, “if continued under the means of grace, may become good corn; therefore have patience with them.”

The witnesses balance the Lord’s forbearance against the duty of discipline. His “Nay” is the patience of one who “can defeat the malice of his foe, but… will choose his own time” (Ellicott) — rather permitting the tares “than any way endanger the wheat” (Henry). Yet this is no licence for laxity: “scandalous offenders are to be censured, and we are to withdraw from them,” the openly wicked “not… admitted to special ordinances.” The caution is against a discipline “so mistaken in its rules, or so over-nice… as may prove vexatious to many that are truly godly.” Clarke turns it sharply against persecution for opinions, “a seed of the devil” no less than a bad opinion itself. And the deepest ground of patience is hope: “the tares, if continued under the means of grace, may become good corn” — the very wheat such a one “would become in time if spared” might be rooted up with him (Augustine).
30:
On the householder's verdict, Let both grow together until the harvest, the grammatical-historical expositors are of one mind that the command governs the visible church for the whole present age and forbids any premature purging. Reading v.30 against its interpretation in v.39, they take the harvest as the end of the world — whether of the Jewish state then near, of the individual's life, or of the present material order — a time fixed and certain though unknown, and, like a literal harvest in summer heat, a season of hurry, of burning wrath, and of the angels' ingathering of the elect from the four winds.

The weight of the comment falls on the pastoral and political lesson. The expositors teach that hypocrites and deceived persons must be expected in the church; that they are the enemy's work and never the fruit of Christianity, which condemns hypocrisy in all its forms; that all hope of removing them entirely is vain; and that an over-zealous attempt to extirpate them injures the true wheat by breeding discord even among believers. The separation is reserved to Christ himself at the proper time. Yet they are careful not to make the parable abolish discipline altogether: it is the duty of the church to keep itself pure and to cut off gross and manifest offenders (1 Corinthians 5:4-5); the toleration enjoined concerns those who may be suspected of hypocrisy but against whom it cannot be proved, who so successfully imitate Christians that no human eye can distinguish them.

From the Wesleyan-Arminian side the same restraint is pressed to its sharpest edge against persecution. Every minister of God should indeed separate the incorrigible from the fellowship, but he should proceed no further: the man is not to be persecuted in body or goods, for false doctrines are against God, who alone is their judge and punisher. The burning of heretics — the fires of the Inquisition, the cruelties under Mary — is named as the very diabolical seed it pretends to root out, an usurpation of the seat of judgment that God has reserved to the last day. The good wheat, meanwhile, is gathered into my barn (Luke 3:17), the granary and storehouse of the saved (1 Timothy 5:24; Malachi 4:1).
31:
The parable of the grain of mustard seed (Mark 4:30-32) is read by every school as the promise that the kingdom, despicable in its beginnings, will grow to an immense and sheltering greatness. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell first on the botany only to dismiss its difficulties: the eastern mustard is not our annual herb but, in a warm climate, grows tall enough that the rabbis spoke of climbing it as a fig-tree, while its seed was proverbially the least of seeds — an apt figure both for the church, at first smaller than any sect in Palestine, Greece, or Italy, and for the convert's faith, at first feeble and easily injured. The single grain is set in pointed contrast with the collective seed of Matthew 17:20.

Charles Ellicott develops the imagery most fully, taking the sower again as the Son of Man but the seed here as the Christian society rather than the bare word, sown in the field of the world to grow till it overtops every kingdom (comparing the cedar-imagery of Ezekiel 31:3 and Daniel 4:10), the birds of the air now no longer emblems of evil but the nations and systems of thought that find shelter under its branches.

The Reformed reading, with Calvin, fixes on the polemical and consolatory point: by these parables Christ braces his disciples not to be offended at the mean beginnings of the gospel. Profane men despise it because its ministers are of low rank and its first disciples few and obscure; weak minds estimate its prospects from its commencement and despair. But the Lord opens his reign with a feeble and contemptible beginning for the express purpose that his power may shine the brighter in its unexpected progress; let believers therefore raise their minds from the flesh's contempt to the boundless power of God, who creates all things out of nothing and daily raises up what exceeds human sense (1 Corinthians 1:28). John Wesley adds the now-familiar division — these parables, unlike the former on unfruitful hearers, concern those who bear good fruit — and reads the kingdom as both the gospel dispensation and the inward kingdom (Luke 13:18). Among the fathers Cyril of Jerusalem stands a little apart, applying the figure not to the church but to faith as a grain of mustard seed (Matthew 17:20): small in size yet fiery in operation, working in the swiftest moment the greatest effects in the soul.
32:
On the seed that is the least of all seeds yet becometh a tree, the comment is largely philological and the schools scarcely divide. The grammatical-historical expositors, led here by John Lightfoot's rabbinic learning, document the proverbial smallness of mustard — according to the quantity of a grain of mustard was the rabbis' phrase for the most diminutive thing — and balance it against the equally rabbinic reports of its prodigious size: the stalk at Sichin with three boughs, one of which roofed a potter's hut and yielded three cabes of seed, and Rabbi Simeon ben Chalaphta's mustard-stalk that he climbed as men climb a fig-tree.

Whether such accounts are exaggerated or not, the commentators agree they could not have arisen unless the plant ordinarily grew large, and Adam Clarke gathers parallels — Herodotus on the gigantic grain of Babylon, his own sight of cabbages nine and fifteen feet high in the Norman isles — to confirm how richly a warm soil and climate raise what a colder region keeps small. The point is conceded on all sides to be popular and not microscopically exact: A.T. Robertson, with Bruce, calls the tree not in nature but in size, “an excusable exaggeration in popular discourse,” and Ellicott notes the phrase means simply greater than the herbs, belonging to a higher order of vegetation. The least, Wesley observes in the Jewish manner, stands for one of the least.

The spiritual application is held in common and stated briefly: as so small a seed becomes so great a plant, so will the Christian doctrine spread through the world and the life of Christ grow in the soul. The botanical detail, in other words, serves a single grounded lesson about disproportionate increase, and the expositors are content to let it rest there without pressing further allegory.

The expositors are content, in the end, to let the disproportion carry the whole weight of the figure and to resist any further allegory. They observe with Gill that mustard was so proverbially small among the Jews that a grain of mustard stood for the least conceivable quantity, and with Wesley that the eastern plant grows exceeding large and high, so that the single comparison — least of seeds, greatest of herbs — needs no decoding beyond itself. The lesson the grammatical-historical commentators draw is therefore single and grounded: as the smallest beginning issues in the largest growth, so the Christian doctrine, planted obscurely, will overspread the world, and the hidden life of Christ in the soul, feeble at first, will rise to great strength and usefulness (Matthew 17:20).
33:
The parable of the leaven divides the church along a clear fault-line between the patristic and the modern readings. The Latin and Greek fathers treat almost every term as an allegory to be decoded, and openly differ over the decoding. Chrysostom reads the leaven as the apostles, who like leaven hidden in meal will change the whole world into their own nature, conquering by being kneaded in rather than destroyed — a word of courage that the very tribulations to come will be the means of their triumph. The three measures are by general patristic consent a figure of totality, but of what they cannot agree: Jerome offers spirit, soul, and body, or reason, anger, and desire after Plato, or even belief in Father, Son, and Spirit fermented into one knowledge of the one Godhead — though he honestly warns that “parables and doubtful solutions of dark things can never bestow authority on dogmas.” Augustine makes the leaven love and the woman wisdom; Hilary, more darkly, makes the woman the synagogue who hides Christ by the sentence of death, the three measures the Law, the Prophets, and the Gospels made one; Raban reads the leaven as love growing until it perfects the whole soul.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed expositors deliberately refuse this method. With Ellicott and Robertson they insist the leaven, though commonly the symbol of malice and corruption (1 Corinthians 5:8; Matthew 16:11), is here frankly turned into a type of pervasive influence for good — Robertson scorning as “exegetical jugglery” the denial that Jesus could so use it, since Scripture likens both the devil and Christ to a lion (1 Peter 5:8; Revelation 5:5). The working differs from the mustard seed: there the growth is outward and visible, here it is inward, secret, and silent, pervading all the faculties of the soul and the institutions of society (Leviticus 23:17). On the three measures these expositors are emphatic that nothing is to be drawn from the number — it is merely the common amount baked at once — and Robertson dismisses both Trinitarian and tripartite readings as “quite beside the mark.” Calvin agrees the term must be understood simply of the present subject. Matthew Henry, mediating, takes the certain, gradual, silent, unstoppable working of leaven as the very manner of the gospel both in the world and in the heart (Mark 4:26-29; Romans 6:13).
34:
That Jesus spake not unto them without a parable draws from the commentators two distinct concerns: the purpose of the method, and a famous textual difficulty in the quotation that follows. On the purpose the schools converge.
