The Greek and Latin fathers, with Chrysostom and his Catena namesake, hold that he taught the multitude in parables as they were able to hear it (Mark 4:33), not to keep them ignorant but to provoke inquiry — and they note with some severity that, though the parables threatened sore punishment, no one asked, whereas the prophets had often been questioned, so that Christ left them and departed. The grammatical-historical expositors qualify the saying carefully: Ellicott observes it is limited by the context to this occasion, but that from this point parables become the dominant element in his public teaching while the mysteries are reserved for the disciples.

The chief debate is over Matthew's ascription of the citation. The text quoted (Psalm 78:2) stands not in Isaiah but in a psalm of Asaph, and the heathen critic Porphyry had already seized on a variant reading by Esaias the prophet as proof of the Evangelist's ignorance. Jerome and Remigius preserve the controversy: Jerome reasons that the original read by Asaph the prophet — Asaph being truly a prophet (1 Chronicles 25:2) — and that a copyist who did not recognize the name substituted the better-known Isaiah, a conjecture Clarke and Ellicott adopt as the likeliest account of the Sinaitic variant. The substance, all agree, is sound: Asaph, like David, ranks among the prophets, and his psalm describes One whose manner of teaching answered to the Psalmist's words. Calvin, declining to make the psalm a peculiar prediction of Christ, holds rather that the same majesty of the Spirit displayed in the prophet's grave and figurative speech was manifested in the Lord's; and that there is no inconsistency with the prophecy of Isaiah already cited, for in withdrawing the light of doctrine from the reprobate Christ yet accommodated himself to their capacity so as to leave them inexcusable. Clarke adds the devotional note that divine things can be taught to man only through the medium of earthly ones, and that herein lies our great privilege.
35:
The formula quotation, I will open my mouth in parables, occasions little division of school and much agreement of substance, the comment running closely parallel to the previous verse. The grammatical-historical expositors trace the words to Psalm 78:2 and identify the prophet as Asaph, expressly so called (1 Chronicles 25:2), defending the ascription against the variant Esaias exactly as before. Ellicott uses the citation to illustrate Matthew's characteristic handling of prophecy (as at Matthew 8:17 and Matthew 12:17): the psalm itself was no prediction but a historical survey of God's dealings with Israel from the Exodus to David, yet the mere occurrence of the word parable at its opening was enough for an Evangelist who excluded chance from such coincidences, so that One whose form of teaching answered to the Psalmist's could claim his words as his own.

On the second clause, things kept secret from the foundation of the world, Robertson catches the force of the verb — I will utter, to gush forth like a river, the passion of a prophet — and finds in it the claim that the life and teaching of Jesus throw a flood of light on purposes of God long kept hidden. Calvin presses the same point against any merely predictive reading: Matthew means not that the psalm peculiarly foretold Christ, but that the loftiness of figurative speech showed Christ was handling the hidden mysteries of God and forbade his doctrine to be despised. Here too Calvin reconciles the apparent tension with Isaiah's prophecy of blindness: though Christ intended to conceal his meaning from the reprobate, he so regulated his style that even in their stupefaction they felt his speech carried some divine and heavenly power. The Gloss, with Anselm, hears Christ himself speaking: the same Lord who once spoke by the prophets now in his own person opens his mouth to bring forth from his secret store mysteries hidden since the world's foundation.

The expositors close, as they began, on the harmony between the Evangelist's purpose and the Psalmist's words. Whether the citation is pressed for its first clause or its second, the grounded point on which Reformed and grammatical-historical voices agree is that Matthew handles the Old Testament not as a quarry of bare predictions but as a witness whose language finds its fullest answer in Christ. The psalm that opened by promising to utter dark sayings of old (Psalm 78:2) is fulfilled when One stands forth whose whole manner of teaching is parable and whose matter is the long-hidden counsel of God; and so, Calvin urges, the very obscurity of the method served to mark the speech as divine, carrying weight and dignity even with those who would not receive it.
36:
The dismissal of the multitude and the disciples' private request to declare the parable of the tares is read by the fathers as a parable of access and by the modern expositors as a lesson in the discipline of learning. Chrysostom and the Catena voices observe that the Lord had spoken in parables precisely to draw out questions; when none came, he sent the crowd away and entered the house, and now the disciples ask freely — the scribes, who had followed only to catch him, not pursuing — passing over the plain parables of the leaven and mustard seed to seek the harder tares. Origen makes much of the geography: when Jesus is with the multitudes he is not in his house, and it is an act of his love of men to leave the house and go out to those who cannot come to him; the genuine hearer follows, inquires after his abode, and abides with him (John 1:35).

The grammatical-historical commentators draw the practical moral. Clarke insists that those who attend only the public preaching are not likely to understand the mysteries of the kingdom; to grasp the divine message a man must come to God by frequent, fervent, secret prayer, by which the word sinks into the heart and bears fruit. Ellicott locates the scene in the house of Peter and reads the request as evidence that the disciples had pondered this parable and found it harder than the rest; Robertson catches the urgency of the aorist explain unto us (so again at Matthew 18:31) — “make thoroughly clear right now.” Albert Barnes, treating the whole interpretation together, stresses that the explanation is so plain as to need little comment: the Son of Man sows the good seed by preaching the gospel through his ministers, providence, and Spirit; the good seed are Christians, the fruit of his toil; and amidst them are the wicked, the work of Satan, who must remain together till the end. He dwells with unusual solemnity on the furnace of fire, arguing that the Redeemer would not hold out frightful images that had no reality — if Christ has spoken of hell, then there is a hell, and the impenitent should be alarmed while the righteous are cheered with the prospect of separation.
37:
On the opening of the interpretation, He that soweth the good seed is the Son of man, the Alexandrian and the Reformed readings stand side by side. Origen, characteristically, extends the figure inward and cosmically: the good seed may be taken as the children of the kingdom because whatever good things are sown in the human soul are the offspring of the kingdom, sown by God the Word who was in the beginning with God, so that wholesome words about anything are children of the kingdom; while men sleep who do not watch and pray (Matthew 26:41), the devil sows evil opinions among the natural good conceptions, and on this reading the whole world, not the church, is the field.

Calvin and the grammatical-historical expositors keep closer to the plain frame of the parable. Calvin notes the unusual mode of expression but takes its plain sense: as in the sowing of fields, so in the preaching of the gospel; and the one peculiarity pointed out is that the tares were sown by the devil's trick. This teaches, first, that the mingling of many wicked men with believers is no accidental or natural occurrence, as though they were the same seed, but must be charged on Satan — not so as to acquit men of guilt, but so that no blame whatever fall on God for a fault that arose from another's agency, and so that we be not surprised to find tares frequently growing in the Lord's field, since Satan is always on the watch to do mischief. Second, Calvin presses that Christ says not that the ministers sow but that he alone sows: though he employs our exertions as his instruments for cultivating his field, he alone acts in and by us, justly claiming for himself what is in some respects common to his ministers. Let us remember, then, that the gospel is preached not only by Christ's command but by his authority and direction; we are only his hand, and he alone is the Author of the work. A misattached fragment of Cyril of Jerusalem here, on the willingness of Christ to go to his passion, belongs to another context.
38:
On the field is the world, the grammatical-historical expositors fasten upon the geography of the parable with unusual precision, for on it turns the whole question of where the tares grow. Robertson presses the Greek article on both nouns, which makes subject and predicate coextensive and interchangeable, and draws the conclusion he thinks “extremely important”: both the good seed and the darnel are sown in the world, not in the Kingdom and not in the church, and the separation comes only at the consummation of the age (v.39), so that they all grow together in the field which is the world. Gill widens the field from the land of Judea, where Christ preached in person, to the whole earth into which the apostles were sent (Romans 10:18; Colossians 1:6), and Wesley simply glosses the good seed as the children of God, the righteous.

On the tares as the children of the wicked one, Ellicott registers and corrects a long interpretive temptation. It was natural, he allows, that theologians who saw in heresy the greatest of evils should identify the tares with heretics; and so far as heresy springs from self-will or antagonism to righteousness, heretics may be included in the class. But he insists the true definition is the one the parable itself supplies in v.41 — all things that offend, and them which do iniquity — a far broader category than doctrinal error, and a caution against making the parable a warrant for persecuting heretics (John 8:44; 1 John 3:8; 1 Peter 1:23). The comment is thin but pointed, and its weight lies in this single corrective: the field is the world, the tares are the wicked broadly, and the verdict is reserved to the harvest.

The grounded sense of the verse, on which the voices agree, is therefore twofold and deliberately broad. First, the arena of the whole drama is the world, not the visible church narrowly conceived, so that the coexistence of good and evil is not a scandal peculiar to the congregation but the very condition of the present age (Romans 10:18). Second, the two seeds are persons and not opinions — the children of the kingdom and the children of the wicked one — which is why Ellicott resists narrowing the tares to heretics and insists on the parable's own definition. The expositors thus read the verse as Christ's own guard against both a sectarian impatience that would purge the church into a remnant and a persecuting zeal that would burn the erring (Colossians 1:6).
39:
The clause the enemy that sowed them is the devil gathers the parable's most extended and consequential reflection, and here the Reformed and grammatical-historical schools speak as one against a hasty zeal. All recognize, with Ellicott, the most distinct acknowledgment of a personal power of evil thwarting God's work, and all dwell on the householder's restraint. Ellicott reads the lesson with great care: the enemy sows while men slept, for the church's time of danger is its hour of apparent security, when watchfulness ceases and errors ripen into heresies; the servants are distinct from the reapers and represent the zealous pastors, whose first impulse to extirpate the evildoers must be checked, since to seek a perfect church that way breeds worse evils than it cures. Beneath the surface lies the latent truth that the tares may by a spiritual transmutation become wheat, so that there is no absolute line of demarcation until the harvest — a rebuke, he adds, to the over-hasty zeal of Puritanism in its many forms and of those who would identify the tares with heretics.

Calvin presses precisely the same point with his own emphasis. The mixture of the reprobate with the church to the very end is a distressing thing, but Christ enjoins patience, not to encourage pollution but to keep weak minds from despair: pastors and all the godly ought to labor strenuously to purify the church, yet they will never make it wholly pure, and those who proceed with undue haste to root out whatever displeases them anticipate Christ's own sentence and usurp the office of the angel-reapers. He turns the verse sharply against the Anabaptists, who abused it to take the sword from the magistrate, replying that the same logic that allows excommunication allows the godly magistrate to use the sword when necessity calls; the parable speaks not of the office of pastor or magistrate at all, but removes the offense felt by those who fancy they may join no society but one as pure as the angels (John 4:38; Matthew 3:12; Matthew 9:37). The harvest is again the end of the age, and the reapers the angels, whose ministry in the final judgment is implied throughout the Lord's greater prophecies (Matthew 25:31; Daniel 7:9-10; Daniel 12:1-2). A misattached fragment of Cyril of Jerusalem, on the signs of the end and the apostles' question, belongs to the Olivet discourse.
40:
The comment on as therefore the tares are gathered and burned is sparse, the expositors treating it as the hinge that turns the explanation toward judgment. Gill simply rehearses the figure as the parable laid it down: in the time of harvest the tares are first gathered out from the wheat, bound in bundles, and burned in the fire prepared for that purpose, so shall it be in the end of the world. The verse carries no controversy and little independent reflection from the grammatical-historical commentators, who reserve their fuller treatment for the verses that name the furnace and the shining of the righteous.

The single notable feature is a stray: the Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note attached here speaks of the healed leper and the command to show himself to the priest (Matthew 8:4), material plainly belonging to an earlier chapter and misplaced in the source. Setting that aside, the grounded sense the voices supply is modest and unanimous: the burning of the tares in the end of this world is the certain, appointed, and final separation, the solemn counterpart to the gathering of the wheat into the barn, and a sober preface to the explicit doom and glory the next verses unfold.

The grammatical-historical expositors, treating the verse as the pivot of the explanation, draw out the few grounded points it carries. The gathering and burning of the tares in the fire is, by the Lord's own key, the appointed end of the world, fixed and certain though its hour is unknown; the binding of the tares in bundles before the burning answers, in the figure, to the final assembling of the wicked for judgment; and the order — the tares first — preserves the parable's earlier insistence that the separation belongs to the harvest and not to the present season. Gill is careful to keep the burning metaphorical of the wrath to come rather than literal, the same restraint the expositors observe at the furnace of v.42. The verse therefore adds no new doctrine but seals the certainty of the consummation, and prepares the reader for the explicit naming of the reapers, the furnace, and the shining of the just that immediately follows (v.43).

It is worth noting, with the expositors, that the misattached note here — on the cleansed leper sent to the priest (Matthew 8:4) — supplies nothing to the sense and is set aside as a stray from another passage, leaving the grounded comment to rest wholly on Gill's plain rehearsal of the figure.
41:
On the Son of man shall send forth his angels, and they shall gather out of his kingdom all things that offend, the grammatical-historical and Reformed expositors press a careful qualification lest the phrase out of his kingdom seem to place the offenders within the church proper. Robertson explains that the gathering is from the midst of the kingdom because in every city the good and the bad are scattered and mixed together (so again from the midst of the righteous in v.49); Jesus does not mean that these stumbling-blocks are actually members of the Kingdom of heaven, but that, as wheat and darnel are mixed in the field, the evil are mixed with the good in the world until the separation comes, their destiny being the furnace of fire. Ellicott marvels that One who stood before men as the carpenter's son speaks of his angels and his kingdom, and explains all things that offend literally as all stumbling-blocks, defined by the following clause as those who work iniquity.

Wesley reads the same clause pastorally: they shall gather all things that offend — whatever had hindered or grieved the children of God, whatever persons or things had kept the good seed from taking root or bearing fruit — the Greek word meaning all scandals (Luke 13:27). Calvin draws from the verse a double admonition. The offenders are so called because their lives are not only wicked but undermine the faith of many, retard others, and draw some entirely aside; this should make us not indolent but zealous and active in guarding against them, and it reproves the delicacy of those whom the smallest stumbling-block makes turn back. Yet our minds ought to be fortified with confidence, for the Son of God, who commands his followers to walk in the midst of scandals, will surely give strength to overcome them all, and pronounces an awful punishment against the hypocrites who now appear the most distinguished citizens of the church. The Cyril of Jerusalem fragment recurs here, again misplaced.

The expositors gather the verse into a single grounded affirmation: the Son of Man, here speaking as the future Judge, owns both the angels who execute the judgment and the kingdom from whose midst the offenders are taken, and the offenders are defined not by doctrinal label but by their effect — all stumbling-blocks, and them which do iniquity. The grammatical-historical and Reformed voices alike are anxious that out of his kingdom not be misread, for the offenders are not true members of the kingdom but are mixed with the wheat in the field of the world until the harvest (v.49). Calvin's pastoral application gives the verse its practical edge: because such offenders undermine the faith of many, the godly must be neither indolent toward them nor so delicate as to be turned back by the smallest scandal, but confident that he who bids us walk among stumbling-blocks will give strength to overcome them all (Luke 13:27).
42:
On shall cast them into a furnace of fire, the commentators agree that the image is metaphorical and the reality it shadows is incomprehensible, and they divide chiefly over how far the figure should be pressed. Gill is explicit that the furnace is not a material but a metaphorical one, denoting the wrath of God that abides on the wicked forever — called elsewhere hell fire and a lake of fire and brimstone, and here a furnace to express the vehemence of the divine wrath, in allusion either to Nebuchadnezzar's furnace, or to the burning of chaff and stubble, after the manner of the Jews, who compared hell to a furnace (Matthew 3:12; Revelation 19:20; Revelation 20:10). Ellicott reads it as the furnace, that of Gehenna, with its wailing and gnashing of teeth (Matthew 8:12).

Calvin makes the metaphor a discipline of sober restraint. As the infinite glory laid up for the sons of God exceeds all our senses and cannot be put into words, so the punishment awaiting the reprobate is incomprehensible and is therefore shadowed out according to the measure of our capacity. From ignorance of this, he says, the Sophists tortured themselves to no purpose with fruitless disputes; and against those commentators who carry ingenious inquiries into every minute phrase, he chooses to philosophize more sparingly and to rest satisfied with the plain and natural meaning, lest subtleties resting on no solid ground lead into idle fooleries. He uses the verse, finally, to answer the curious who ask how, while Christ slept and knew nothing of it, the devil could sow the tares: they will find nothing to reply, but his own caution has left out nothing useful or necessary to be known. The unanimous grounded point is thus restraint — the furnace is a true and dreadful warning, expressed in earthly terms because the thing itself surpasses the human mind.

The grounded consensus of the voices is therefore that the furnace is a true and dreadful reality, expressed in earthly figure because the thing itself surpasses the human mind. The grammatical-historical expositors anchor the image in its scriptural and Jewish background — the burning of chaff, Nebuchadnezzar's furnace, the lake of fire (Revelation 20:10) — while refusing to decide whether the fire is material, holding only that no fiercer figure was available to convey the suffering. Calvin's restraint becomes itself a doctrine: as the glory of the saints is shadowed because it exceeds our senses, so the punishment of the reprobate is shadowed because it is incomprehensible, and the wise interpreter will philosophize sparingly and rest in the plain and dreadful meaning rather than torture the phrase into idle curiosities (Matthew 8:12).
43:
On then shall the righteous shine forth as the sun in the kingdom of their Father, the comment turns from terror to consolation, and the Reformed and grammatical-historical voices alike find in it the climactic comfort of the parable. The image draws by common consent on Daniel's promise that the wise shall shine as the brightness of the firmament and as the stars (Daniel 12:3), and Robertson catches the verb's force — to shine out as the sun emerges from behind a cloud once the separation has driven away the darkness. Gill dwells on the contrast with the present: the righteous, who in this world were loaded with reproaches, afflicted, and despised for their meanness, shall then shine in the robe of Christ's imputed righteousness, in perfect holiness, and in the glorified, incorruptible body, like Christ the Sun of righteousness himself, without spot or cloud. Ellicott notes that the crowning blessing is that this is the kingdom of their Father (Proverbs 4:18).

Calvin develops the verse as a remarkable consolation and reads its single emphatic adverb, then, as an implied contrast between the saints' present obscurity and their ultimate restoration. Though many wicked men now hold high rank in the church, that blessed day is assuredly to be expected when the Son of God will raise his followers on high and remove everything that dims their brightness. The future glory is promised, he insists, only to those in whom the image of God already shines and who are being transformed into it by continual advances; but because their life is now hidden and their salvation invisible, consisting in hope, Christ directs their eyes to heaven where the promised glory is laid up. Borrowing openly from Daniel, Calvin reasons that the prophet who foretells a future brightness intimates a present obscurity; and by comparing the glory to the sun Christ does not make it equal in all, for as he now distributes his gifts variously, so will he variously crown them. The inheritance of the godly is set against the earth to remind them they are here but pilgrims (Luke 17:21; 1 Corinthians 15:28). A Cyril of Jerusalem fragment on the resurrection body, citing this same shining of the righteous, stands here a little apart from the parable's frame.
44:
The treasure hid in the field opens the second triad of parables, and the schools divide sharply over what the treasure and the field signify, while agreeing wholly on the lesson of all-surpassing worth. The fathers allegorize freely. Chrysostom takes the treasure as the gospel preaching, hidden in this world and not to be purchased but by selling all, and that with joy. Hilary makes the treasure free in the finding (the preaching open to all) but costly in the possessing, since heavenly riches are not had without the loss of this world. Jerome offers a double reading — the treasure is Christ the Word hidden in the flesh, or the Scriptures in which the knowledge of the Saviour is laid up (Colossians 2:3). Gregory reads the hidden treasure as the desire of heaven and the hiding as the guarding of holy zeal from the robbery of human praise. Augustine finds in it the two Testaments of the Church; Origen questions whether these house-spoken similitudes are parables at all.

Matthew Henry and the grammatical-historical expositors set the allegory on a firmer evangelical footing. Henry, treating all four parables together, makes Jesus Christ himself the true Treasure, in whom is all fulness (Colossians 1:19; John 1:16) and all treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3), and the gospel the field in which he lies hid — an open field, where whoever will may search the Scriptures and dig (Proverbs 2:4; John 5:39). Those who discern the treasure and value it aright will never rest till they make it their own on any terms, selling all to buy the field. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson read the treasure as a buried hoard or hidden mine, and freely concede that the finder's concealment from the owner does not square with strict integrity; but, as with the Unjust Steward, the parable does not concern itself with such questions and presses only the man's earnestness and his readiness to sacrifice all (Luke 14:33; Philippians 3:8). Calvin and Wesley draw the same lesson inward: the kingdom within is a treasure hid from the world's wise, and he who finds it hides it deep in his heart and gives up all other happiness for it.
45:
The pearl of great price is read by every school as a near-twin of the treasure, the dream doubled because the thing is certain, yet the commentators differ over whom the merchant and the pearl represent. The patristic readings again multiply. Chrysostom makes the one pearl the truth, which is one and not manifold, and the merchant the believer who must be both detached from this life's business and watchful. Jerome takes the goodly pearls as the Law and the Prophets and the one supreme pearl as the knowledge of the Saviour and the sacrament of his passion and resurrection, which when found makes the seeker count all else as dung (Philippians 3:8) — not that the old pearls are condemned, but that beside this one they are worthless. Gregory makes the pearl the sweetness of the heavenly kingdom; Augustine, with characteristic depth, makes it Christ the sinless One, or love of neighbour in which all is comprehended (Romans 13:9), or the Word in whom all things are contained (John 1:1) — and adds the searching thought that the preciousness we buy with the pearl is the possession of ourselves, which we cannot have until we despise all that this world can possess. Origen, learned in the lore of pearls, makes the prophets the mussels conceiving the heavenly dew and the one costly pearl the Christ, on whose finding all the rest are easily gathered.

The grammatical-historical and evangelical expositors keep to the plain point and mark one suggestive difference from the treasure. Barnes and Clarke take the parable to teach that salvation is of more value than all things and worth the sacrifice of all, Clarke citing the Talmudic tale of Joseph the Sabbath-keeper and the pearl found in the fish; Ellicott grounds the figure in the Roman luxury that made pearls the type of all costliest adornment (Matthew 7:6; 1 Timothy 2:9). The marked difference, Ellicott and Wesley observe, is that here the search is presupposed: the man has already been seeking goodly pearls of wisdom, holiness, and truth, and is then led to the higher knowledge of Christ for which he resigns all — the history of Paul (Philippians 3:8), of Justin and Augustine, of every faithful seeker who, willing to do God's will, comes to know the doctrine (John 7:17).
46:
On the merchant who, having found the one pearl, went and sold all that he had, and bought it, the comment is brief and the schools undivided, the verse being read as the decisive act to which the whole parable has moved. Robertson catches the vividness of the Greek tenses — the dramatic perfect he has gone off and sold, then the bought it, present perfect and imperfect and aorist together for lively action — and notes that the word for merchant denotes a travelling trader who goes in and out like a drummer. The point all draw is the totality and eagerness of the surrender: nothing the man owns is too great a price for the surpassing wealth of the kingdom.

Calvin guards the verse against a legalist misreading. By the word bought Christ does not mean that men bring any price with which to purchase the heavenly life, for we know on what free condition the Lord invites believers (Isaiah 55:1); though the heavenly life and all that belongs to it is the free gift of God, we are yet said to buy it when we cheerfully relinquish the desires of the flesh that nothing may prevent us from obtaining it, as Paul reckoned all things loss and dung that he might gain Christ (Philippians 3:8). The stray Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note here, on the Gentiles from Tyre and Sidon and Christ's kinsmen thinking him beside himself (2 Corinthians 5:13; Matthew 12:46), is misplaced from an earlier passage. Setting it aside, the grounded sense is single: salvation is free in its giving and yet costs the renouncing of all that competes with it.

The grounded point on which the few voices here agree is the completeness and gladness of the surrender, set against any notion that the kingdom is bought by merit. Robertson's lively tenses capture the eager haste of the merchant who, having found the one pearl, loses no time in turning all his lesser goods into the price of it; and Calvin's careful gloss keeps the act from legalism, for the heavenly life remains the free gift of God (Isaiah 55:1) and is bought only in the sense that the believer cheerfully relinquishes every competing desire to obtain it. So the parable, in its closing act, teaches both the incomparable worth of Christ and the wholehearted renunciation of all that would keep us from him, exactly as Paul counted his former gains but loss and dung that he might win Christ (Philippians 3:8).
47:
The parable of the dragnet is read by every school as a companion to the tares, and the commentators agree on its central lesson while differing over its precise edge. Chrysostom marks the difference between the two parables with care: in both some perish and some are saved, but in the tares men are lost for heresy of evil dogmas, in the sower for not attending to the word, and here for evil life — for though drawn by the net, that is, enjoying the knowledge of God, they cannot be saved without good living; and the parable is added precisely lest men trust in preaching only, or think bare faith sufficient. Jerome makes the net the gospel woven of the Old and New Testaments, cast by the apostle-fishermen into the sea of this world (Jeremiah 16:16), gathering of every kind until it is drawn to shore at the end. Gregory likens the net to the Holy Church given into the fishers' hands, drawing every sort of man — wise and foolish, bond and free — out of the waves, full only when the sum of the human race is complete.

The grammatical-historical expositors keep to the plainer figure and stress the same two thoughts as in the tares: the present mingling of evil with good in the visible kingdom, and the final separation. Robertson identifies the sagenè as the great drag-net, not the hand-net of Matthew 4:18, sweeping in all the fish so that, as the field is the world, the net catches all in the sea, the separation coming afterward. Barnes presses the apologetic point that the most striking pictures of hell fall from the lips of Christ himself, and asks how this agrees with those who say all will be saved (Matthew 22:10). Clarke and Ellicott add the suggestive qualification that the very casting of the net may change the nature of the fish, so that those who remain bad are so in the end by their own will. Calvin finds the parable's design partly the same as the tares — to remove the offense of an impure church — and partly distinct: to restrain the disciples from resting in the empty profession of faith, for it is of little consequence to be gathered into the fold unless we are Christ's true and chosen sheep (2 Timothy 2:19; 1 Corinthians 14:33).
48:
On the net when it was full, drawn to shore that the good might be gathered into vessels and the bad cast away, the comment is brief and the schools undivided, the verse being treated as the mechanism of the separation already promised. Gill reads the fulness ecclesiologically: the gospel and the gospel church-state are full when all the ends of the preaching are answered, when all are called who were designed to be called, when the fulness of the Gentiles is brought in and all Israel shall be saved (Romans 11:25). The drawing to shore is the consummation, the sorting the final judgment.

The grammatical-historical note here is merely lexical: Robertson observes that the word for vessels (angè) occurs only here in the New Testament, distinct from the form used of the wise virgins' vessels in Matthew 25:4. The verse carries no controversy; its grounded sense, supplied chiefly by Gill and confirmed by the figure itself, is that the church's mixed condition endures only until the net is full, and that the careful gathering of the good into vessels — as valuable and to be kept — answers to the gathering of the wheat into the barn, while the casting away of the bad answers to the burning of the tares. The fuller doctrinal weight the expositors reserve for the next verse, where the parable is explicitly applied to the end of the world.

The grounded weight of the verse, for the expositors, lies in its place within the parable's larger lesson rather than in any detail of its own. The drawing of the full net to shore is the consummation toward which the whole present age is tending, and the careful gathering of the good into vessels answers, in this companion parable, to the gathering of the wheat into the barn in the tares (v.30): the good are kept as valuable and the bad cast away as worthless. Gill's ecclesiological reading — that the net is full when the fulness of the Gentiles is brought in (Romans 11:25) — gives the verse its forward look toward the completed church, while the grammatical-historical commentators are content to note that the sorting on the shore is the work of the next verse, where the figure is plainly applied to the end of the world. The verse thus marks the moment of transition from the present mixed condition to the final separation, and adds nothing to the doom or the glory but the certainty that the net will indeed one day be drawn full to land.
49:
On so shall it be at the end of the world: the angels shall come forth, and sever the wicked from among the just, the commentators are content to let the Lord's own interpretation stand, and the grammatical-historical expositors note chiefly the parallel with the tares. Gill draws out the figure: as the fisherman, when the net is drawn to shore, picks out the good fish into vessels and casts away the dead and useless, so at the close of time the angels execute the separation. Barnes underscores that the severing is from among the just (so again the gathering out of the midst in v.41), and that the ministry of the angels in this is expressly affirmed (Matthew 25:32), as it was in the parable of the tares where Christ likewise assigned the work to the angels rather than performing it alone.

Matthew Henry, treating the dragnet's interpretation, gives the verse its sharpest pastoral application. The angels of heaven shall come forth to do what the angels of the churches — the ministers — could never do: they shall sever the wicked from among the just, and since they have both their commission and their instructions from him who knows all men and particularly knows them that are his, there shall be no mistake or blunder either way. The single Cyril of Jerusalem fragment attached here, on the signs of the end and the apostles' question about the day of Christ's coming, belongs properly to the Olivet discourse and is a stray. Setting it aside, the unanimous grounded sense is that the present indistinguishable mixture in the visible church will be resolved with perfect, unerring justice at the last day, by the ministry of angels and at the command of the all-knowing Judge.

The grounded consensus of the voices is that the verse simply ratifies, in the figure of the fishery, the same final judgment already taught in the tares, with the same insistence on the angels' ministry and the same assurance of unerring justice. Henry's note that the angels do what the angels of the churches cannot is the verse's sharpest pastoral application: the minister labours among a mixed multitude he cannot infallibly sort, but the heavenly reapers act on the commission and knowledge of him who knows them that are his, so that no sincere soul will be lost in the casting away and no hypocrite preserved in the gathering (Matthew 25:32). The present indistinguishable mixture, the expositors conclude, is therefore no ground for despair but a call to patience until the shore is reached.
50:
The repetition of the doom — and shall cast them into the furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth — is passed over briefly by the expositors, who simply refer the reader back to the identical words in v.42 and to the furnace-imagery treated there. Gill and Barnes both cross-reference their own earlier comment, and Clarke sends the reader to his note on Matthew 8:12 for the meaning of the weeping and gnashing. The grammatical-historical voices thus take the verse as a deliberate refrain, the dragnet ending where the tares ended, so that the two parables close on one note of solemn warning.

The only extended material here is a stray fragment of Cyril of Jerusalem, which quotes the furnace-language of Matthew 6:30 in the course of refuting Marcion and the heretics who divided the good God from the just Creator — matter belonging to the catechetical lectures, not to this parable. The grounded sense the voices supply is therefore minimal and unanimous: the casting of the bad fish into the furnace is the same final judgment already pronounced on the tares, and its deliberate repetition is itself the point. Henry's observation on the parallel verse holds here too — that Christ himself preached often of hell-torments as the everlasting punishment of hypocrites, and it is good for us to be often reminded of this awakening, quickening truth.

The grounded sense, on which the voices agree, is that the deliberate repetition is itself the lesson: the dragnet is brought to close on the very words that closed the tares (v.42), so that the two parables of the mixed kingdom end on one solemn and identical warning. The grammatical-historical expositors decline to add fresh comment precisely because the refrain is meant to be heard as a refrain, the second sounding of a note already struck driving the warning the deeper. Henry's observation on the parallel verse is the fitting gloss here too — that Christ himself preached often and plainly of the everlasting punishment of hypocrites, and that it is good for the soul to be frequently reminded of this awakening and quickening truth (Matthew 8:12).
51:
The question Have ye understood all these things? and the disciples' Yea, Lord are read by the fathers as a lesson in the dignity of the apostolic office and by the modern expositors as a model of teachable learning. Jerome and Hilary take the disciples to be addressed as those who must not merely hear, like the multitude, but understand, as having to teach others — the apostles being the Saviour's notaries, who wrote his precepts on the fleshly tables of the heart, and so are fittingly called scribes instructed (Song of Solomon 7:13). Origen probes the form of the question, holding that Christ, though he knew the things in men's hearts (John 2:25), asks as a true man, using the human characteristic of inquiry, as the God of the universe asked Adam, where art thou? and Where is Abel thy brother? (Genesis 3:9; Genesis 4:9).

The grammatical-historical and Reformed expositors read the exchange as instruction in the duty of understanding. Barnes notes the kindness of the question — if they had not understood, Christ was still willing to teach them — and that the truth they had gained should not lie unemployed but be brought forth as a householder brings from his store. Clarke insists that divine truths must not be lightly passed over but considered again and again till thoroughly understood; Ellicott hears in the disciples' answer both a grateful consciousness of rapid growth in insight and a certain child-like simplicity in their readiness to claim that they had mastered all the mysteries. Calvin reminds us that all the parables were explained in private, and that Christ warns by this question that his object in teaching them so carefully was not merely that they might be well informed but that they might communicate to others what they had received — whetting their minds the more to desire instruction. The Gloss adds simply that they were instructed only so far as they understood, which is why he asks.

The grounded point the expositors draw is that understanding is the proper end of hearing and the necessary condition of teaching. The kindness Barnes notes in the question — that Christ was ready to explain further had they not understood — meets the warning Calvin presses, that the careful private instruction was given them not to be hoarded but to be communicated to others; and Ellicott's gentle observation on the disciples' ready Yea, Lord reminds the reader that a real but still child-like grasp of the mysteries is the seed from which the instructed scribe of the next verse will grow (v.52). The exchange thus stands as a small model of discipleship: the Master examines, the learner answers honestly, and the knowledge gained is held in trust for the good of others.
52:
The saying about the scribe instructed unto the kingdom of heaven who brings from his treasure things new and old draws a striking measure of agreement across the schools, all reading it as Christ's commendation and commission of the teacher, with one persistent point of debate. The fathers fasten on the order of the words. Augustine notes pointedly that Christ said new and old, not old and new, preferring the order of value to the order of time, and turns this against the Manichaeans who keep only the new and so put on a newness of error. Gregory and Hilary debate what the new and old denote: Gregory rejects the reading of the two Testaments (since then Abraham and Moses, who had not the New, could not be learned householders) and takes them rather as the sweetness of the kingdom and the terror of punishment, that fear may deter whom reward does not draw; or as the preaching of both Testaments in word and life. Hilary takes them as Law and Gospel from the one householder's treasury of the Holy Spirit.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed expositors read the verse as the charter of the gospel ministry. Clarke makes the scribe the minister taught of God, whose treasury is the Scriptures, and presses the duty of distribution: a good pastor, unlike the miser or the merchant, brings forth with a liberal yet judicious hand and suits his discourse to his hearers — he who preaches the same sermon to every congregation proves he is no scribe instructed in the kingdom. Ellicott values the verse as one of the few where Christ compares his own work and the apostles' to that of the scribes, training the unlearned to be his successors, and finds the union of new and old exemplified supremely in his own teaching — the old eternal laws of righteousness joined to new truths of his mission and the Spirit's work — the secret at once of reverence for the past and courage for the future. Robertson contrasts the merely Rabbinical scribe, who produces only the stale, with the disciple of the kingdom, always fresh-minded yet valuing the old treasures; Calvin and Wesley read it of the teacher who lays up a store by long study to draw out, as from a granary, instruction fitted to every need (2 Timothy 3:16-17).
53:
The transition when Jesus had finished these parables, he departed thence, and the rejection at Nazareth that follows, are read by the fathers and the harmonists with two concerns: the chronology, and the meaning of the townsmen's unbelief. The grammatical-historical expositors and Augustine note the looseness of the connection: Augustine observes that Matthew passes to this event without evident sequence, while Mark and Luke order things differently, and Calvin and Ellicott infer that some interval elapsed and that the notes of time are not to be pressed (Luke 4:16; Mark 6:1). On the place, Chrysostom and Origen identify his own country as Nazareth, not Capernaum, where the mighty works were done (Matthew 11:23).

The substance of the comment is the folly of the Nazarenes' offense. Jerome exclaims at their wonderful folly — they marvel whence Wisdom itself has wisdom and Power its mighty works, yet stumble because they take him for the carpenter's son, and being mistaken in his Father are mistaken too in his brethren, whom he and the Catena fathers (with Augustine) explain as kinsmen, the sons of the other Mary, on the customary view that guards the perpetual virginity. Chrysostom presses the perversity: they ought to have honoured him the more that one of such parentage and no schooling spoke so wisely, as David sprang from a husbandman and Amos from a shepherd; their contempt showed his wisdom came not of human industry but of divine grace. Matthew Henry develops the same with his usual warmth: Christ came again to those who had rejected him, not taking refusers at their first word; they upbraided him with his want of academical education and the poverty of his relations, turning that against him which was really for him, for had they not been wilfully blind they must have concluded him divinely commissioned who without education gave such proofs of wisdom and power. Pseudo-Augustine and Hilary lift the carpenter image to the Father who framed all the works of nature, and to the Son who subdues the rude mass of the world to every use. The grounded verdict, shared by all, is that familiarity bred contempt — a prophet is honoured save in his own country — and that their unbelief, not any want of power in Christ, tied his hands (Luke 2:34; Isaiah 8:14).
54:
On the Nazarenes' astonished question, Whence hath this man this wisdom, and these mighty works?, the commentators agree that the wonder was just but the inference perverse, and they divide only in emphasis. Origen reads the wonder as unwitting testimony: there was indeed a great and surpassing wisdom in the words of Jesus, worthy of a greater than Solomon is here (Matthew 12:42), and works greater than those of Elijah, Elisha, Moses, and Joshua; yet supposing him the son of Joseph the carpenter, they depreciated his whole kindred. He notes the tradition, from the Gospel of Peter or the Book of James, that the brethren were Joseph's sons by a former wife — a view several of the fathers share.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed expositors press the culpability of the unbelief. Barnes and Clarke infer from the townsmen's surprise that Jesus had lived in obscurity until then, his neighbours having neither heard his doctrine nor seen his miracles before; Clarke adds the melancholy truth that those who should know Christ best are often most ignorant of him. Calvin makes the verse a study in willful blindness: they are struck that one untaught and long employed in a mechanical occupation should be so eminent a teacher, and in this they ought to have perceived the hand of God; but their ingratitude dazzled their understanding, so that they derived no profit from what was before their eyes. They are compelled to admire him whether they will or not, and yet they despise him — which is nothing else than to reject a prophet whom God has taught because he was not educated by men. They cut their own throats, Calvin says, by their very acknowledgment, rendering so honourable a testimony to a doctrine that has no influence on them, because it does not take its origin in the usual way from the earth. They ought rather to have lifted their eyes to heaven and learned that what exceeds human reason must have come from God, and to have been the more powerfully affected that miracles were added to the doctrine; instead they maliciously drew a veil over their own eyes, searching out grounds of offense to avoid the path to which God invited them. Wesley adds only the practical caution that many texts are misunderstood for want of the proper emphasis — here on whence hath HE (Mark 6:1; Luke 4:16).
55:
The taunt Is not this the carpenter's son? — in Mark, the carpenter himself — provokes the commentators both to historical note and to a frank confessional debate over the brethren. The grammatical-historical expositors take the trade plainly and turn it to honour. Gill records the ancient tradition that Joseph was a carpenter and Jesus brought up to the same craft of ploughs and yokes, citing the Antioch schoolmaster's retort to Libanius that the carpenter's son was making a coffin for Julian. Barnes argues from Mark's wording that Jesus had worked at the business until he was thirty, a useful and honourable industry, and rebukes the infidel who sneers at the Son of God in a humble trade while praising Peter the Great for learning ship-carpentry; it was the Jewish custom to train every child to a manual occupation (so Paul the tent-maker, Acts 18:3). Ellicott adds Justin Martyr's report that ploughs and yokes said to be made by him were shown as relics in his day.

On the brethren the church divides openly, and the expositors register both sides. Clarke and Wesley represent the two readings most sharply. Clarke argues, against what he calls a determined prejudice of Papists and Protestants alike, that plain common sense takes James, Joses, Simon, and Judas, with the sisters, to be the children of Joseph and Mary — the Scriptures being silent on the perpetual virginity (Matthew 1:25). Wesley, by contrast, takes them as kinsmen, the sons of the other Mary, wife of Cleophas, James being called the Lord's brother by Paul (Galatians 1:19). Calvin holds the Hebrew idiom by which brethren denotes any relatives, and judges Helvidius to have shown excessive ignorance in concluding that Mary must have had many sons. Robertson states the plain reading — younger half-brothers and sisters, sons of Joseph and Mary — while the fathers (Jerome, with the Catena) maintain the cousins or step-children view. The grounded common point is that the offense was the meanness of his family, not any real disgrace; an honest trade is no discredit, and they should have respected him the more as one of themselves.
56:
The clause and his sisters, are they not all with us? is touched only lightly by the voices, and chiefly to identify the sisters and to register, once more, the confessional question of the kinship. Gill reports, from Epiphanius, the tradition that the sisters were named Mary and Salome, and notes the two views he has already canvassed — that they were Joseph's daughters by a former wife, or the daughters of Alphaeus and Cleophas — without himself adjudicating. The verse carries no independent doctrine; Barnes simply refers it back to his comment on the brethren in v.55.

The single force the expositors draw from the detail is the one Matthew Henry pressed on the whole list: that the very nearness of these relations, the sisters being all with us and known to every neighbour, was turned by the Nazarenes into a reason for contempt rather than respect. They should have loved and honoured him the more because he was one of themselves; instead his ordinary kindred became the occasion of their stumbling. The grounded sense, supplied by Gill's identification and Henry's application, is therefore that the sisters' familiar presence in the town deepened the offense — the same prejudice that despised the carpenter's son and his brethren despised him also for the obscurity of his sisters, measuring worth by extraction and rank, which the expositors with one voice call a poor standard by which to judge.

The grounded force of the verse, slight as its independent matter is, lies in the cumulative effect the expositors trace through the whole catalogue of the family. The Nazarenes recite his mother's common name, his brethren's names, and his sisters' familiar presence among them, and at every point turn nearness into contempt; the very evidence that should have endeared him — that he was wholly one of themselves, reared in their streets — becomes the occasion of their unbelief. Henry's verdict gathers the sense: they measured worth by extraction and rank, which the expositors with one voice call a poor and deceiving standard, and so stumbled at the obscurity of the kindred through whom the Wisdom and Power of God had come among them (v.55; Isaiah 53:2).
57:
On they were offended in him, and the proverb A prophet is not without honour, save in his own country, the commentators agree closely both on the nature of the offense and on the saying's force, with only minor differences of scope. The grammatical-historical and Reformed expositors locate the stumbling in pride and envy. Gill suggests they could not believe he had his wisdom from himself or from God, and so, rather than own a divine mission, chose to suspect he had it from the devil, or else took offense at the meanness of his birth and education. Barnes makes it the envy of equals: men always look with envy on those of their own rank who advance pretensions to uncommon wisdom, and the Nazarenes were too proud to be taught by one whose humble life and family they knew — a case he likens to Columbus, honoured everywhere but in his own country. Clarke and Robertson press the same: their pride and envy were the causes of their destruction, and it was held unpardonable in Jesus not to be commonplace like themselves (Matthew 11:6; John 6:42; Isaiah 53:3).

On the proverb itself there is a small debate of range. Origen asks whether the saying holds universally of every prophet — objecting that Elijah, Elisha, Samuel, and Jeremiah did not in fact suffer dishonour in their own birthplaces — and so concludes it is true only figuratively, taking Judaea as the prophets' country and the body as their house, or else applies it singularly to the Saviour. Calvin, with Chrysostom, agrees the proverb may be general but was applied by Christ with peculiar reference to his Galilean countrymen, who instead of being the first to accept the grace offered them drove him away; it is the standing ingratitude of men that, the more familiarly God exhibits himself to them, the more boldly they reject him in the influences of his Spirit (John 4:44; Luke 4:24). Clarke offers the modest alternative that Christ may mean himself alone — his ministry more received in any other part of the land than among his own relatives, who, knowing his obscure birth, could scarcely suppose he had these things from heaven.
58:
On he did not many mighty works there because of their unbelief — in Mark, more strongly, he could do no mighty work there — the commentators are remarkably united, finding in the verse a real and instructive limitation of the wonder-working power, conditioned upon faith. The grammatical-historical expositors note both the fact and its measure. Barnes and Ellicott explain that the power was not absolute and unconditioned but depended on the faith of those who came, so that the will and the power were alike thwarted; Mark adds that he yet laid his hands on a few sick folk and healed them, so that he did some works, both to leave the people inexcusable and that it might not be said he wished his own country no good. Robertson puts it plainly: the disbelief of the townspeople blocked the will and the power of Jesus to work cures. Gill stresses that what few works he did were such as raised their wonder yet, through the hardness of their hearts, became rather stumbling-blocks than grounds of faith.

Origen and Calvin draw out the principle most fully. Origen marks the precise word — not he would not but he could not — and reasons that the working power received a co-operation from the faith of the one on whom it worked, a co-operation hindered by unbelief, as the disciples could not cast out the demon for their little faith and Peter began to sink for his doubt (Matthew 17:20; Matthew 14:31), while the woman with the issue of blood, who only reasoned that a touch would heal her, was healed at once (Matthew 9:21). Calvin, with the same care, holds that the impiety of his countrymen closed the door against more miracles: faith is like the open mouth of a vessel and unbelief the stopper that shuts out the liquor God would pour in. Yet he notes that Matthew says not many, not none — the goodness of Christ strove with their malice and triumphed over every obstacle, opening a path for itself where none existed; an amazing contest, that while men labour by every method to hinder the grace of God, it still rises victorious. Clarke, citing Quesnel, adds that unbelief and contempt drive Christ out of the heart as they drove him from his own country, and Henry that unbelief is the great obstruction to Christ's favours, so that if mighty works be not wrought in us, it is not for want of power in Christ but of faith in us (Mark 9:23; Romans 1:16; Ephesians 2:8).
